Shallow ReLUs Networks inL p-Type and Sobolev Spaces: Approximation and Path-Norm Controlled Generalization∗ W eizhao Liweizhaoli24@m.fudan.edu.cn School of Mathematical Sciences Fudan University Shanghai, China F anghui Liufanghui.liu@sjtu.edu.cn School of Mathematical Sciences Institute of Natural Sciences and MOE-LSC Shanghai Jiao Tong University Shanghai, China Lei Shileishi@fudan.edu.cn School of Mathematical Sciences Shanghai Key Laboratory for Contemporary Applied Mathematics Fudan University Shanghai, China Abstract We study approximation by shallow ReLU s networks,σ s(t) = max{0, t}s, and the gener- alization behavior of such networks underℓ 1 path-norm control. For theL p-type integral spaces eFp,τd,s, 1≤p≤2, we prove approximation bounds for shallow networks using spher- ical harmonic analysis. In particular, when the parameter measure is the uniform measure τd andp < p∗ = 2d+2 d+3 , we obtain the rateO  m− 1 2 − d(2−p) 2d(2−p)+2p(2s+d+1) log 3 2 m  , which im- proves the corresponding random-feature rate. We also derive approximation rates for Sobolev spacesW α,p in the range 1≤p <2 by embedding them into spectral Barron spaces. Finally, for nonparametric regression with sub-Gaussian noise, we prove minimax- optimal generalization bounds for path-norm-regularized shallow ReLU s networks: the rates areO  n− d+2s+1 2d+2s+1 logn  overB s andO  n− 2α 2α+d logn  overW α,∞, with matching lower bounds up to logarithmic factors. Keywords:Neural Networks, Sobolev Spaces, Variation Spaces, Path Norm 1 Introduction Deep learning has shown remarkable effectiveness in high-dimensional approximation prob- lems, particularly in scientific computing, inverse problems, and operator learning (Han et al., 2018; Adcock et al., 2022; Beck et al., 2023). In many such settings, the ReLU s activationσ s(t) = max{0, t}s (s∈N 0) is especially relevant because it yields piecewise- polynomial representations that are well suited to smooth targets and derivative-sensitive tasks (Yang and Zhou, 2025; He et al., 2024). ∗. Authors are listed in alphabetical order and contributed equally to this work. Corresponding authors: Fanghui Liu and Lei Shi. 1 arXiv:2605.18468v1 [stat.ML] 18 May 2026

To obtain meaningful approximation guarantees, one must first identify an appropriate function class. Reproducing kernel Hilbert spaces (RKHSs) are a standard choice, but they cannot effectively approximate even a single ReLU neuron (Yehudai and Shamir, 2019). More fundamentally, Steinwart (2024) proved that on an uncountable compact space, no RKHS contains all continuous functions. This result highlights an intrinsic limitation of RKHSs: they are naturally adapted to smooth functions and therefore have limited expres- sive power for nonsmooth targets. Sobolev spacesW α,p, by contrast, accommodate such nonsmooth functions and are central in approximation theory and the analysis of partial differential equations (Triebel, 1983). However, Sobolev spaces are also too large from a statistical perspective:anymachine learning method is statistically ineffective over the full Sobolev class (Wainwright, 2019). This raises a basic theoretical question:which function classes can neural networks learn efficiently, and how does that answer depend on the acti- vation function and the regularity of the target space?E et al. (2022) reformulated Barron spaces (Barron, 1993) and showed that, for shallow ReLU networks, the Barron space is exactly the largest function class that can be learned statistically efficiently. 1 The connection between RKHSs and Barron spaces can be made precise through the Lp-type spaces studied by Celentano et al. (2021) and Chen et al. (2025). These works consider the following infinite-width ReLU s model: f(x) = Z V a(w, b)σs  w⊤x+b  dν(w, b), whereV=S d−1 ×[−1,1] is the parameter space for (w, b) and dνis a probability measure onV. Ifa(w, b)∈L p(dν), thenfbelongs to anL p-type space, denoted byf∈ eFp,ν,s (with formal definition in Section 2). In particular, eFp,ν,s reduces to an RKHS whenp= 2, while the Barron space is close to a variational space (Bach, 2017) and can be identified with the union of a family ofL p-type spaces (Chen et al., 2025; Celentano et al., 2021). By H¨ older’s inequality, for 1≤p≤2, eF2,ν,s ⊆ eFp,ν,s ⊆ eF1,ν,s , so these spaces form a natural bridge from RKHSs to Barron spaces. The connections among these spaces can be conceptually described by Figure 1. TheL p-type spaces describe the size of the neural-network integral representation, whereas Sobolev spaces describe classical smoothness. The two are connected through embedding theorems: suitable Sobolev regularity implies membership in a spectral Barron or relatedL p-type representation space, which then yields shallow-network approximation rates. Precise definitions of these function spaces are given in Section 2. We study the limits of approximating functions inL p-type spaces and Sobolev spaces by shallow ReLUs networks. For widthm, let Σs m =   fm = 1 m mX j=1 ajσs  w⊤ j x+b j  :a j ∈R,w j ∈R d, bj ∈R    , 1. If a function belongs to the Barron space, then shallow ReLU networks can learn it without suffering from the curse of dimensionality; conversely, if a function can be sufficiently learned by shallow networks without the curse of dimensionality, then it must belong to the Barron space. 2

RKHS, eF2,ν,s Barron spaceseFp,ν,s Sobolev spaces Statistically efficient Statistically inefficient Figure 1: Hierarchy of function spaces considered in this paper, ordered from smaller to larger in generality. and let Σ s = S∞ m=1 Σs m denote the space of shallow ReLU s networks of arbitrary width. Studying approximation over eFp,ν,s clarifies how the difficulty of learning changes along the spectrum from RKHSs to Barron spaces. For Sobolev spaces, the theory is relatively complete whenp≥2, whereas the regime 1≤p <2 remains much less understood. This regime is important in PDE analysis and inverse problems with nonsmooth or singular structure: solutions with shocks, edges, or sparsity often belong naturally toW α,p for 1≤p <2, but need not lie in the Hilbert-type spaceW α,2. Extending approximation guarantees to this setting is therefore relevant both theoretically and practically. 1.1 Related W ork We first discuss previous work on approximation overL p-type spaces and Barron spaces, and then give an overview of generalization results for shallow ReLU s networks related to such spaces. For approximation overL p-type spaces, the existing literature covers only the two end- point casesp= 1 andp= 2 (Bach, 2017). In the intermediate regime 1< p <2, Chen et al. (2025) studied random feature models over eFp,ν,s through a dual framework link- ing approximation and estimation, and obtained the improved approximation upper bound O(m−1+ 1 p log 3 2 m) for random feature models. That framework, however, does not extend directly to general shallow networks. On the other hand, Bach (2017) and Siegel (2025) studied approximation of eF1,ν,s via zonoid and zonotope theory, but their arguments rely heavily on the equivalent integral representation (1), which makes extension to generalp difficult. In Sobolev approximation, a central challenge is to measure the regularity of the target in anL q norm while deriving error bounds in anL p norm. Whenq≥p, optimal approximation rates are known; whenq < p, the problem becomes substantially harder and the available results are limited. Using the embeddingW s+ d+1 2 ,2 ,→B s, Mao et al. (2026); Siegel and Xu (2024) established the estimate inf fm∈Σsm ∥f−f m∥L∞ ≲∥f∥ W s+ d+1 2 ,2m− 1 2 − 2s+1 2d . This result shows that regularity measured inL 2 can still yield an optimal approximation rate inL ∞, providing the first result in the regimep > q. However, it covers only the parameter settingq= 2,p=∞, andα=s+ d+1 2 ; other regimes remain open. In particular, 3

whenα > s+ d+1 2 , one can derive only the upper boundO  m− 1 2 − 2s+1 2d  by combining embeddings with known approximation results onB s. More precisely, for 2≤p≤ ∞, s∈N 0,α >0, andf∈W α,p, one has inf fm∈Σsm ∥f−f m∥Lp ≲∥f∥ W α,p ( m− 1 2 − 2s+1 2d , α≥s+ d+1 2 , m− α d , α∈N∩ � 0, s+d+1 2  . Whenα < s+d+1 2 , achieving the optimal rate requiresαto be an integer, and the noninteger case remains open. Moreover, the available theory applies only to 2≤p≤ ∞, mainly because Mao et al. (2026) relies on the spectral Barron spaceB s as an intermediate space, while the embeddingW α,p ,→B s generally fails for 1≤p <2. We address this regime by establishing new embeddings between Sobolev spaces and spectral Barron spaces. There is also substantial literature on the generalization behavior of shallow ReLU s networks. Bach (2017) and Parhi and Nowak (2023) systematically analyzed infinite-width ReLU models. In particular, Bach (2017) proved a representation theorem for integral ReLU networks with variational-norm regularization, implying that a finite-width network fm can always be trained, and established the generalization upper boundO  n− 1 2  over the variational space eF1,ν,1. Parhi and Nowak (2023) used Barron regularization to show that the minimax-optimal rate over Barron spaces isO  n− d+3 2d+3  . For finite-width net- works, Liu et al. (2024) explored the trade-off between sample complexity and dimension dependence, and obtained the upper boundO  n− d+2 2d+2  over Barron spaces underℓ 1 path norm constraints, although that rate is not minimax optimal but the dimension depen- dence is relaxed. By contrast, Yang and Zhou (2024) consideredℓ 2 path norms and proved minimax-optimal ratesO  n− d+3 2d+3  andO  n− 2α 2α+d  over Barron and H¨ older spaces, respec- tively. Since theℓ 2 path norm leads to a formulation equivalent to∥ · ∥2-regularized learning (Yang and Zhou, 2024), its analysis is substantially cleaner than that of theℓ 1 counterpart. 1.2 Contributions Our results focus on shallow ReLU s networks. We prove approximation results overL p- type spaces eFp,ν,s, extend the Sobolev approximation theory toW α,p when 1< p <2, and establish minimax-optimal generalization bounds over Barron and Sobolev spaces. The main contributions are as follows: •Using spherical harmonic analysis, we establish new approximation results for shal- low ReLU s networks overL p-type spaces eFp,ν,s. In particular, when the parameter measure is the uniform distributionτ d andp < p∗ = 2d+2 d+3 , the approximation rate is O  m− 1 2 − d(2−p) 2d(2−p)+2p(2s+d+1) log 3 2 m  , improving upon the rate obtained by random fea- ture models in Chen et al. (2025). Thus, optimizing the inner parameters of a shallow network can improve approximation over random feature models for relatively harder target classes. •By establishing embeddings between spectral Barron spaces and Sobolev spaces, we derive approximation rates for shallow ReLU s networks overW α,p when 1< p <2. 4

In particular, whenα≥s+d, the rate isO  m− 1 2 − s−1 d+1 logm  ; whenα∈N∩(0, s+d), the rate isO  m− α(2s+d+3) 2(s+d)(d+1) logm  . •We prove that for anys∈N 0, shallow ReLUs networks withℓ 1 path norm constraints achieve minimax-optimal generalization bounds over both Barron and Sobolev spaces. Our analysis builds upon techniques from (Yang and Zhou, 2024; Wainwright, 2019). The framework of Yang and Zhou (2024) applies only to bounded or Gaussian noise; here we extend the generalization bounds to sub-Gaussian noise distributions using generic chaining. The rest of the paper is organized as follows. Section 2 reviews the shallow-network model and the relevant Barron,L p-type, and Sobolev spaces. Section 3 presents the main approximation results. Section 4 establishes minimax-optimal generalization bounds. Sec- tion 5 concludes the paper. Appendix A collects the notation used throughout. 2 Preliminaries We recall the function spaces and learning framework used in the approximation and gen- eralization results. 2.1 F unction Spaces Barron spacesConsider functions of the following form: fρ(x) =E (a,w,b)∼ρ h aσs  w⊤x+b i ,x∈Ω, where dρis a probability measure on the Borelσ-algebra. Such functions are precisely infinite-width shallow ReLU s networks. Define thesth-order Barron spaceby Bs ={f=f ρ :ρis a probability measure}, and define thesth-order Barron normoff ρ by ∥fρ∥Bs = inf   E(a,w,b)∼ρ[|a|(∥w∥ 1 +|b|) s] :f=f ρ . Here,B 1 is exactly the Barron space introduced in E et al. (2022), for which optimal approximation rates and metric-entropy estimates are already known (Siegel and Xu, 2024). For generals∈N 0, one can show thatB s is equivalent to the variation space defined in Siegel and Xu (2024); as a consequence, the same approximation and entropy estimates remain available. This equivalence is one byproduct of the present work. Lemma 1Lets∈N 0. Then for anyf∈B s, one has inf fm∈Σsm ∥f−f m∥L∞ ≲∥f∥ Bs m− 1 2 − 2s+1 2d . The proof of this lemma relies on the equivalence between the Barron space and the variation space, which allows us to invoke the corresponding known results directly. Details are given in Appendix B. 5

Lp-type spacesThe study of networks in integral form can be traced back to kernel methods and random feature models. If a signed Radon measure admits a densitya(w, b) with respect to a fixed probability measureν, then the collection of all functions satisfying Z V |a(w, b)|2 dν(w, b)<∞ forms a reproducing kernel Hilbert space (RKHS) (Bach, 2017; Celentano et al., 2021; Chen et al., 2025), with reproducing kernel k(x,x ′) = Z V σs  w⊤x+b  σs  w⊤x′ +b  dν(w, b). Although kernel methods are mathematically well developed, they suffer from the curse of dimensionality. For example, they struggle to learn ridge functions that depend on only a few input coordinates. To mitigate this limitation, one can generalize the RKHS construction. Since an RKHS is essentially a weightedL 2 space, Bach (2017) introduced the corresponding weightedL 1 space in the ReLU setting, namely the class of functions satisfying R V |a(w, b)|dν(w, b)<∞, often called convex neural networks. Celentano et al. (2021); Chen et al. (2025) then considered the general casep≥1, leading to the family of Lp-type spaces eFp,ν,s =  f(x) = Z V a(w, b)σs  w⊤x+b  dν(w, b) :a(w, b)∈Lp(dν)  , equipped with the norm ∥f∥ eFp,ν,s = inf  ∥a(w, b)∥Lp(dν) :f(x) = Z V a(w, b)σs  w⊤x+b  dν(w, b)  . For a finite network fm(x) = 1 m mX j=1 ajσs  w⊤ j x+b j  , the corresponding discrete control is the generalizedℓ 1 path norm ∥fm∥P1,s = 1 m mX j=1 |aj|(∥w j∥1 +|b j|)s . This quantity is the atomic, finite-width analogue of the Barron or variation norm: replacing the finite sum by a signed measure gives the integral representation underlying Barron spaces. This link motivates the path-norm control used in the generalization analysis below. Sobolev spacesSobolev spaces play a central role in areas such as partial differential equations. We use the standard definition based on weak derivatives. For 1≤p≤ ∞, the Sobolev spaceof orderαis defined by W α,p(Rd) = n f∈L p(Rd) :∥f∥ W α,p(Rd) <∞ o , where: 6

1. Whenαis an integer, ∥f∥ W α,p(Rd) = X |β|≤α ∥∂βf∥ Lp(Rd) ; 2. Whenαis not an integer, writeα=k+θ, wherekis a nonnegative integer and θ∈(0,1). Then ∥f∥ W α,p(Rd) = X |β|≤k ∥∂βf∥ Lp(Rd) + X |β|=k ∂βf(x)−∂ βf(y) ∥x−y∥ d p +θ 2 Lp(Rd×Rd) . RestrictingW α,p(Rd) to Ω yields the corresponding restricted Sobolev spaceW α,p(Ω). 2.2 Background: Generalization Analysis Unless otherwise specified, the input domain is Ω =B d (all results can in fact be extended to general compact sets with Lipschitz boundary), and the label space isZ⊆R. Let (X, Y) follow the true distribution dρon Ω×Z, and suppose that we observeni.i.d. noisy samples Dn ={(x i, yi)}n i=1. The goal of theregression problemis to learn, fromD n, a function fm ∈Σ s that approximates the regression function fρ(x) =E[Y|X=x] = Z Y ydρ(y|x),x∈Ω, whereρ(· |x) denotes the conditional distribution ofYgivenX=x. It is standard to assume thatf ρ belongs to a hypothesis spaceH, such as a Barron space (Liu et al., 2024) or a Sobolev space (Yang and Zhou, 2024, 2025), reflecting prior knowledge about the target. Besides fitting the data well, neural-network training must also control model complex- ity in order to ensure good generalization. In the overparameterized regime, the classical viewpoint that measures complexity by parameter count is no longer adequate; the double- descent phenomenon (Belkin et al., 2020; Hastie et al., 2022) is a standard example. This has motivated a norm-based view of training, in which the optimization procedure is understood as explicitly or implicitly minimizing a suitable weight norm, often called a representation cost (Neyshabur et al., 2015; Ongie et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2024; Yang and Zhou, 2024). We adopt explicit regularization and consider the followingL 2 empirical risk minimiza- tion problem: fm ∈arg min f∈Σ s ( 1 n nX i=1 |f(x i)−y i|2 +λ∥f∥ ) , whereλ≡λ(n)>0 is the regularization parameter and∥ · ∥denotes a weight norm of the network. We choose the (generalized)ℓ 1 path norm (E et al., 2022; Liu et al., 2024): ∥f∥ P1,s = 1 m mX i=1 |ai|(∥w i∥1 +|b i|)s , 7

which can be viewed as a discrete analogue of the Barron norm. Denote by Σ s m,M the set of shallow ReLUs networks withmneurons andℓ 1 path norm at mostM. Note that the path norm is not parametrization invariant: two different parameterizations of the same network function may have different path norms. This dependence on representation is precisely why the path norm provides a meaningful measure of model complexity. We consider the expected generalization error of the estimatorf m, EDn h ∥fm −f ρ∥L2(dµ) i , where dµis the marginal law ofX. If one can prove an upper bound together with a matching minimax lower bound, then the estimator is statistically optimal. 3 Approximation Results We now present approximation results for shallow ReLU s networks over theL p-type space eFp,τd,s and the Sobolev spaceW α,p. The first result treatsL p-type integral spaces with optimized inner parameters. The second concerns Sobolev spaces in the range 1≤p <2, where Hilbert-space methods based onL 2 regularity no longer apply directly. 3.1 Approximation ofL p-type Spaces We first study approximation of the integral representation class eFp,τd,s, where the param- eter measure is the uniform measureτ d on the sphere. Previous results for the intermediate range 1< p <2 were mainly obtained for random feature models, in which the inner parameters are sampled and then fixed. Here the inner parameters are optimized as part of the shallow network, and this additional flexibility leads to a better approximation rate whenpis below the critical indexp ∗. Theorem 2 (Approximation ofL p-type spaces by shallow networks)Letτ d be the uniform measure onS d. Ford≥2,s∈N 0,1≤p≤2, andf∈ eFp,τd,s, the following bound holds for everym∈N: inf fm∈Σsm ∥fm −f∥ L2 ≲    m− 1 2 − d(2−p) 2d(2−p)+2p(2s+d+1) log 3 2 m ,1≤p < p ∗ , m− 1 2 log2 m , p=p ∗ , m− p(2s+d+1) p(d+3)−2(d+1)+2p(2s+d+1) log 3 2 m , p∗ < p≤2, wherep ∗ = 2d+2 d+3 . The proof of Theorem 2 is based on an intermediate approximation step. Instead of approximatingf∈ eFp,τd,s directly by a finite network, we first approximate it by a function in the Hilbert-type space eF2,τd,s and then approximate this intermediate function by a shallow network. The first step is where theL p structure enters. We begin on the sphere. After writing the parameter asθ= (w ⊤, b)∈S d, the uni- form measureτ d allows us to diagonalize the integral operator associated withσ s(θ⊤u) by spherical harmonics. This gives the following approximation result. 8

Lemma 3 (Approximation on the unit sphere)Letd≥2,s∈N 0,1≤p≤2, and ˜f∈ eFp,τd,s(Sd). Assume that ˜fhas parity opposite to that ofs. Then, for everyR >0, inf ∥g∥ eF2,τd,s(Sd)≤R ∥ ˜f−g∥ L2(Sd) ≲∥ ˜f∥ eFp,τd,s(Sd)    R− 2s+d+1 d · p 2−p ,1≤p < p ∗ , Re−(s+ d+1 2 )R2 , p=p ∗ , R− 2p(2s+d+1) p(d+3)−2(d+1) , p ∗ < p≤2, wherep ∗ = 2d+2 d+3 . The key point of Lemma 3 is that theL p integrability of the outer coefficienta(θ) can be converted into decay of the spherical-harmonic components of ˜f. The boundedness range of the spherical-harmonic projection operators is exactly what produces the critical index p∗. The next step transfers the spherical approximation back to the original domain. We use the lifting ˜f(u) =u s d+1f  u′ ud+1  ,u= (u ′, ud+1)∈S d, u d+1 >0, and then restrict the spherical approximant back to Ω. Corollary 4 (F romS d toB d)Letd≥2,s∈N 0,1≤p≤2, andf∈ eFp,τd,s. Then, for everyR >0, inf ∥g∥ eF2,τd,s ≤R ∥f−g∥ L2 ≲∥f∥ eFp,τd,s    R− 2s+d+1 d · p 2−p ,1≤p < p ∗ , Re−(s+ d+1 2 )R2 , p=p ∗ , R− 2p(2s+d+1) p(d+3)−2(d+1) , p ∗ < p≤2, wherep ∗ = 2d+2 d+3 . Combining Corollary 4 with the available approximation bound for functions in eF2,τd,s gives the final network approximation. Since a random-feature model is a special shallow network, (Chen et al., 2025, Theorem 6.3) implies that, if∥g∥ eF2,τd,s ≤R, then there exists fm ∈Σ s m satisfying ∥g−f m∥L2 ≲Rm − 1 2 log 3 2 m . Balancing this term with the intermediate error in Corollary 4 gives the three cases in Theorem 2. The proofs of Lemma 3, Corollary 4, and the final balancing argument are given in Appendix C. The proof of Theorem 2 uses the special structure of the uniform measureτd in Lemma 3. For a general measureτ, the Funk–Hecke formula is no longer available, so one cannot derive a spherical-harmonic expansion analogous to (4). Moreover, even when dν≪dτ d, the Radon–Nikodym derivative need not inherit the integrability ofa(w, b), so the proof strategy breaks down. The critical valuep ∗ is also tied to the boundedness of projection operators: the argument requires estimates for the modulus of smoothness onL p-type spaces, and theL p boundedness of spherical-harmonic projection operators changes at a 9

critical index (Kwon and Lee, 2018), producing the logarithmic factors in the bound. We conjecture that this phase transition is intrinsic rather than an artifact of the proof. Theorem 2 shows that, away from the critical pointp ∗, the approximation rate depends monotonically onp: largerpleads to better approximation over eFp,τd,s. Whenp= 1, the rate over eF1,τd,s isO  m− 1 2 − d 4d+4s+2 log 3 2 m  ; lettingd→ ∞yields the dimension-independent boundO  m− 3 4 log 3 2 m  , which is better than the known rate over the Barron spaceB s. This is consistent with the inclusion relationB s = S ν eFp,ν,s, and suggests thatB s is strictly larger than any fixedL 1-type space. Whenp= 2, eF2,τd,s reduces to an RKHS, and the rate becomesO  m− 1 2 log 3 2 m  asd→ ∞, matching the rate for random feature models. Although shallow networks form a strictly larger class than random feature models and might therefore seem capable of better approximation, the Monte Carlo lower bound of orderm − 1 2 (Lin, 2017) shows that no further improvement is possible in this case. At the critical pointp=p ∗, the rate is againO  m− 1 2 log2 m  , indicating that shallow networks have essentially the same approximation power over theL p∗ -type space as over an RKHS. 3.2 Approximation of Sobolev Spaces We next turn to Sobolev spaces. Existing shallow-network approximation results for Sobolev functions often pass through embeddings into Barron or spectral Barron spaces, but the available embeddings are most effective when the smoothness is measured inL 2 or stronger norms. For 1≤p <2, such an embedding is more delicate. The following theorem gives an approximation bound by first embeddingW α,p into a spectral Barron space and then applying known spectral-Barron approximation estimates. Theorem 5 (Approximation of Sobolev spaces by shallow networks)Lets∈N 0, α >0,1≤p <2, andf∈W α,p. Then inf fm∈Σsm ∥f−f m∥Lp ≲∥f∥ W α,p    m− 1 2 − s−1 d+1 logm , α≥s+d , m− α(2s+d+3) 2(s+d)(d+1) logm , α∈N∩(0, s+d). Theorem 5 addresses the regime 1≤p <2, which is not covered by Mao et al. (2026). Its proof is based on a new embedding from the Sobolev spaceW α,p into the spectral Barron space, which reduces the problem to known approximation results for the latter. Since the currently available approximation rate over the corresponding spectral Barron space is not sharp, the bound in this theorem is not expected to be optimal either. The proof is given in Appendix D. 4 Generalization Analysis The Barron norm is equivalent to the variation norm of infinite-width ReLU representations, while theℓ 1 path norm is its finite-width, parameter-level counterpart. This connection pro- vides a natural way to control the complexity of trained networks through their parameters and motivates the path-norm regularization used below. We consider empirical risk mini- 10

mization with anℓ 1 path-regularization term: fm ∈argmin fθ∈Σsm ( 1 n nX i=1 |fθ(xi)−y i|2 +λ∥θ∥ P1,s ) . For simplicity, we assume throughout that the above optimization problem admits a solu- tion. 4.1 Upper Bounds for the Generalization Error We derive upper bounds for the generalization error of shallow ReLU s networks under suitable regularity and noise assumptions. We begin with the following two assumptions: Assumption 6 (Regularity assumption)The regression function satisfies fρ ∈W α,∞(Ω),∥f ρ∥W α,∞(Ω) ≤1, whereα >0and, whenα < s+ d+1 2 ,αis an integer. Assumption 7 (Noise assumption)Letη=Y−f ρ(X)denote the (zero-mean) noise. Thenηis sub-Gaussian, that is, there existsσ >0such that E h eλη i ≤e λ2σ2 2 ,∀λ∈R. Moreover, it is independent ofX. In Assumption 6, the requirementα∈Nwhenα < s+ d+1 2 comes from the approxima- tion theorem used later. Assumption 7 is more general than the commonly used bounded- noise assumption: it includes bounded, Gaussian, and more general sub-Gaussian noise. An even broader condition is the sub-exponential assumption, namely, that there existR >0 andC >1 such that E[|η|p|X=x]≤Cp!R p,∀p∈N, x∈Ω, for which analyses of unbounded errors can be found in (Wainwright, 2019; Guo and Zhou, 2013; Adamczak, 2008). Both the sub-Gaussian and sub-exponential conditions can be unified by requiring the Orlicz norm∥ · ∥ψp to be finite. It is defined by ∥X∥ ψp = inf{t >0 :E[exp(|X| p/tp)]≤2}, p≥1. Whenp= 1 andp= 2, this reduces to the sub-exponential and sub-Gaussian cases, respec- tively. The main result of this subsection is the following theorem, proved in Appendix E. Theorem 8Under Assumptions 7 and 6, consider the empirical risk minimizer fn ∈arg min f∈Σ sm ( 1 n nX i=1 (f(x i)−y i)2 +λ∥f∥ P1,s ) . Then there exist constantsc 1, c2 >0such that: 11

1. Ifα≥s+ d+1 2 , then by taking m≳n d 2d+2s+1 , λ≍n − d+2s+1 2d+2s+1 logn one has ∥fn −f ρ∥2 L2(dµ) ≲n − d+2s+1 2d+2s+1 logn with probability at least1−c 1e−c2n d 2d+2s+1 logn . 2. Ifα∈N∩(0, s+ d+1 2 ), then by taking m≳n d 2α+d , λ≍n − 1 2 − 2s+1 4α+2d logn one has ∥fn −f ρ∥2 L2(dµ) ≲n − 2α 2α+d logn with probability at least1−c 1e−c2n d 2α+d logn . 4.2 Minimax Lower Bounds The goal of this subsection is to show that the upper bound in Theorem 8 is minimax optimal. We continue to consider i.i.d. samples{(x i, yi)}n i=1 drawn from an unknown distribution dρ, and estimators of the form fm ∈arg min f∈Σ sm ( 1 n nX i=1 (f(x i)−y i)2 +λ∥f∥ P1,s ) . For the lower-bound argument, we impose the following two assumptions: Assumption 9 (Noise assumption)The samples satisfy yi =f ρ(xi) +η i, η i ∼ N(0, σ2). Hereσ 2 >0is the variance. Assumption 10 (Sample distribution assumption)Assume that the marginal distri- bution ofXis the uniform distribution onΩ, namely,µ=U(Ω). Assumption 9 is a special case of Assumption 7. We impose Gaussian noise mainly because it simplifies the computation of the Kullback–Leibler divergence. Similar arguments should also apply to certain other sub-Gaussian distributions, such as log-concave or light- tailed laws. Assumption 10 is introduced because we need to estimate the metric entropy logN(ε, Wα,∞(Ω),∥ · ∥L2(dµ)). Ifµ=U(Ω), then logN(δ, BWα,∞(Ω),∥ · ∥L2(dµ))≍logN(δ, BW α,∞(Ω),∥ · ∥L2(Ω)). 12

In fact, it is enough to assume thatµ≪mand thatw= dµ dx is bounded, wheremdenotes Lebesgue measure; under this condition, the same conclusion continues to hold. Given a target function classF, we define theminimax riskby inf ˆf sup fρ∈F E h ∥ ˆf−f ρ∥2 L2(dµ) i , where the infimum is taken over all estimators, that is, all measurable functions of the sample{(x i, yi)}n i=1. We are interested in lower bounds of ordern −β. If such a lower bound matches the upper bound in Theorem 8, then the estimatorf m is statistically optimal. Theorem 11Letα >0. Then under Assumptions 9 and 10, the minimax risk satisfies inf ˆf sup fρ∈W α,∞(Ω), ∥fρ∥W α,∞(Ω)≤1 E h ∥ ˆf−f ρ∥2 L2(dµ) i ≳n − 2α 2α+d . It follows that whenα < s+ d+1 2 , the upper bound in Theorem 8 matches the min- imax lower bound up to logarithmic factors, so the estimatorf n is statistically optimal. By contrast, whenα≥s+ d+1 2 , the upper bound no longer matches the Sobolev min- imax lower bound. The reason is that our upper-bound argument uses the embedding W α,∞(Ω)⊂B s(Ω), which effectively reduces the problem to the Barron class and yields the raten − d+2s+1 2d+2s+1 logn. The next theorem shows that this rate is itself minimax optimal overB s(Ω). Theorem 12Under Assumptions 9 and 10, the minimax risk satisfies inf ˆf sup fρ∈Bs(Ω), ∥fρ∥Bs(Ω)≤1 E h ∥ ˆf−f ρ∥2 L2(dµ) i ≳n − d+2s+1 2d+2s+1 . 5 Conclusion and Discussion We studied approximation and generalization for shallow ReLUs networks. For theL p-type space eFp,τd,s, 1≤p≤2, spherical harmonic analysis gives approximation rates for shallow ReLUs networks (Theorem 2), with a phase transition atp ∗ = 2d+2 d+3 . For Sobolev spaces W α,p with 1≤p <2, we obtainedL p approximation rates (Theorem 5) through embeddings into spectral Barron spaces, covering a regime not treated in Mao et al. (2026). On the generalization side, we analyzed nonparametric regression withℓ 1 path-norm- regularized shallow ReLU s networks under sub-Gaussian noise, using generic chaining. When the regression functionf ρ belongs to the Barron spaceB s, the generalization er- ror satisfies the upper boundO  n− d+2s+1 2d+2s+1 logn  ; whenf ρ ∈W α,∞, it satisfies the upper boundO  n− 2α 2α+d logn  . Combined with the corresponding minimax lower bounds (Theo- rems 11 and 12), these rates are minimax optimal up to logarithmic factors in their respective settings. Several open problems remain. 13

•Optimality of approximation rates in Sobolev spaces.The rates in Theorem 5 for 1≤p <2 are not yet optimal. The main obstruction is that the currently available approximation rate over the corresponding spectral Barron space is itself not sharp. It would be interesting to develop more refined harmonic-analysis tools, such as Littlewood–Paley decompositions, to analyze approximation inW α,p directly and possibly obtain optimal rates. •Approximation under general parameter measures.The approximation results forL p-type spaces in Theorem 2 rely on taking the parameter measureνto be the uniform distributionτ d, which makes the Funk–Hecke formula available. For general probability measuresν, the present proof no longer applies. Extending the theory to broader classes of parameter measures remains an open problem. •Approximation lower bounds.The present work establishes only upper bounds for approximation overL p-type spaces. Matching lower bounds, and hence minimax- optimal approximation results, remain open. Deriving such lower bounds would com- plete the picture behind Theorem 2. The techniques developed here may also be useful for studying deeper architectures, where the interaction between representation norms, approximation, and statistical com- plexity remains less understood. Appendix C. Proof of Theorem 2 This appendix follows the approach of (Bach, 2017; Yang and Zhou, 2025) to prove Theorem 2. We first transform the problem into an approximation problem on the sphere. To use spherical harmonic analysis, we take the parameter space in this section to beS d, instead ofS d−1 ×I, and write the parameter asθ= (w ⊤, b)∈S d. We begin with a brief review of harmonic analysis on the sphere (Dai and Xu, 2013). Fori∈N 0, the space of spherical harmonics of degreei,Y i, is the linear space of restrictions toS d of real homogeneous harmonic polynomials of degreeionR d+1. The dimension ofY i is N(d, i) := ( 2i+d−1 i �i+d−2 d−1  , i̸= 0 1, i= 0. Spherical harmonics are eigenfunctions of the Laplace-Beltrami operator ∆: ∆Yi =−i(i+d−1)Y i, Y i ∈Y i ,(2) where, in coordinatesu= (u 1, . . . , ud+1)∈S d, ∆ = dX k=1 ∂2 ∂u2 k − dX k=1 dX ℓ=1 ukuℓ ∂2 ∂uk∂uℓ −d dX k=1 uk ∂ ∂uk . Define the inner product⟨g 1, g2⟩= R Sd g1(u)g2(u)dτd(u) onS d; then spherical harmonics of different degrees are orthogonal with respect to this inner product. Anyg∈L 2(Sd) has a unique decomposition inL 2(Sd): g(u) = Z Sd g(v)dτ d(v) + ∞X i=1 N(d, i) Z Sd g(v)P i(u⊤v)dτd(v) =: ∞X i=0 gi(u), whereP i are the Gegenbauer polynomials: Pi(t) := (−1)i 2i Γ(d/2) Γ(i+d/2) (1−t 2)(2−d)/2  d dt i (1−t 2)i+(d−2)/2, t∈[−1,1], which satisfy∥P i∥L∞[−1,1] ≤1 and 1−P i(cosβ)≍min{1, i 2β2}. The above decomposition can be viewed as an extension of Fourier series to the sphere. Forg∈L 2(Sd), denote byg i its projection onto the spherical harmonic space of degreei. We also have the Parseval identity on the sphere: ∥g∥2 L2(Sd) = ∞X i=0 ∥gi∥2 L2(Sd) .(3) A useful observation is that ifg∈ eFp,τd,s andg(u) = R Sd a(θ)σs(θ⊤u)dτd(θ), then the Funk– Hecke formula (Bach, 2017) gives the Fourier series decomposition g(u) = ∞X i=0 λiai(u),(4) whereλ i denotes the Fourier coefficient ofgwith respect to the Gegenbauer polynomial: λi := ωd−1 ωd Z 1 −1 g(t)Pi(t)(1−t 2) d−2 2 dt . 18

Proof of Theorem 8 Before proving this theorem, we need some preparatory results. Define the empirical distribution associated with the sample{(x i, yi)}n i=1 by µn = 1 n nX i=1 δxi , which induces the corresponding empiricalL 2(dµn) norm ∥f∥ 2 L2(dµn) = Z Ω |f(x)| 2dµn(x) = 1 n nX i=1 |f(x i)|2 . The upper bound for the generalization error depends on the size of the chosen neural-network model class, so we need a notion of model complexity. The local complexity defined below is the main tool in the error analysis. Definition 15Given a model classF, define its local complexity by Gn(F, δ,ξ) =E ξ " sup f∈F,∥f∥ L2 (dµn )≤δ 1 n nX i=1 ξif(x i) # , δ >0, where{ξ i}n i=1 is a sequence of independent identically distributed zero-mean random variables. Whenξ i =η i, we abbreviate it asG n(F, δ); this quantity measures the correlation between the model classB ∂Σsm (r) and the noiseηon the sample{x i}n i=1. Whenξ i are Rademacher or Gaussian random variables,G n(F, δ,ξ) reduces to the classical local Rademacher or local Gaussian complexity, respectively (Wainwright, 2019). The value ofG n(δ,ξ) depends on the sample{x i}n i=1, but we suppress this dependence because the complexity bounds below hold uniformly over all samples. A set is calledstar-shapedif for anyfin the set and anyt∈[0,1], we also havetfin the set. For a non-star-shaped setF, we may define its star-shaped hull by star(F) ={tf:f∈ F, t∈[0,1]}. Lemma 16LetFbe star-shaped. Viewing Gn(F,δ,ξ) δ as a function ofδ, it is monotonically decreas- ing on(0,+∞). Therefore, for any constantc >0, δ∗ n = min{δ >0 :G n(F, δ,ξ)≤cδ 2} always exists and is finite. ProofWrite the local complexity simply asG n(δ). For any 0< δ≤t, we show thatG n(t)≤ t δ Gn(δ). For anyf∈ Fwith∥f∥ L2(dµn) ≤t, by star-shapedness we have δ t f∈ Fand δ t f L2(dµn) ≤δ. Hence 1 n nX i=1 ξif(x i) = t δ 1 n nX i=1 ξi · δ t f(x i) ≤ t δ sup f∈F,∥f∥ L2 (dµn )≤δ 1 n nX i=1 ξif(x i) . By the arbitrariness off, it follows that sup f∈F,∥f∥ L2 (dµn )≤t 1 n nX i=1 ξif(x i) ≤ t δ sup f∈F,∥f∥ L2 (dµn )≤δ 1 n nX i=1 ξif(x i) . 27

Before formally deriving the upper bound on the error, we first briefly describe the proof strategy. We begin with a decomposition of the excess error. LetB=B(m)>sup x∈Ω |fρ(x)|>0 be a truncation constant to be determined. For anyf∈Σ s m, consider ∥πBfm −f ρ∥2 L2(dµ) ≤2∥πBfm −π Bf∥ 2 L2(dµ) + 2∥πBf−f ρ∥2 L2(dµ) ≤2∥π2B(fm −f)∥ 2 L2(dµ) + 2∥f−f ρ∥2 L2(dµ) , where we have used|π Bfm −π Bf| ≤ |π2B(fm −f)|andB >sup x∈Ω |fρ(x)|. The second term on the right-hand side can be controlled directly by the approximation theorem 5. For the first term, following the method of (Wainwright, 2019, Chapter 13,Chapter 14), we first estimate the empirical error∥π 2B(fm −f)∥ 2 L2(dµn) under a fixed-design setting, and then use a uniform law of large numbers to pass from the fixed-design setting to the random-design setting, thereby obtaining an upper bound on∥π 2B(fm −f)∥ 2 L2(dµ). For the empirical error∥π 2B(fm −f)∥ 2 L2(dµn), note that for anyf∈Σ s m, ∥π2B(fm −f)∥ 2 L2(dµn) ≤ ∥fm −f∥ 2 L2(dµn) ≤2∥fm −f ρ∥2 L2(dµn) + 2∥f−f ρ∥2 L2(dµn) , Again, the second term on the right-hand side can be controlled directly by Theorem 5, so it remains only to estimate the empirical error∥f m −f ρ∥2 L2(dµn) of the empirical risk minimizerf m, which is precisely the core of the upper-bound analysis. We now carry out the complete proof in three steps. Step 1: Upper bounds on the empirical error and theℓ1 path norm In this subsection, we fix the sample{(x i, yi)}n i=1 and analyze upper bounds for the empirical error and theℓ 1 path norm of the empirical risk minimizerf m. For anyf∈Σ s m, by definition, 1 n nX i=1 (fm(xi)−y i)2 +λ∥f m∥P1,s ≤ 1 n nX i=1 (f(x i)−y i)2 +λ∥f∥ P1,s . 30

Noting thaty i =f ρ(xi) +η i, substituting this and rearranging yields thebasic inequality ∥fm −f ρ∥2 L2(dµn) +λ∥f m∥P1,s ≤∥f−f ρ∥2 L2(dµn) +λ∥f∥ P1,s + 2 n nX i=1 ηi(fm(xi)−f(x i)). From this basic inequality and the oracle inequality to be derived below, we can obtain upper bounds for both the empirical error and theℓ 1 path norm. To state and prove this oracle inequality, we need a tail bound for the Gaussian empirical process. Theorem 18LetM, δ >0and define the sub-Gaussian empirical process Z(δ) = sup f∈star(∂(Σ s m)), ∥f∥ L2 (dµn )≤δ 1 n nX i=1 ηif(x i) , Then there exist constantsL, L′ >0depending only on the Gaussian constantσsuch thatZsatisfies the concentration inequality P  Z(δ)≥ L√n γ(δ) +t  ≤L ′e− nt2 4L2 δ2 ∀t >0. Yang and Zhou (2024) established generalization results under theℓ2 path norm only for bounded noise. For bounded noise or Gaussian noise, one may directly use the classical Hoeffding inequality or Gaussian concentration inequality to obtain similar conclusions. Here we provide the proof under sub-Gaussian noise. In this case, the previous two concentration inequalities are no longer applicable, so we introduce the finer quantityγ(δ) to characterize the concentration property of the empirical process. This completes the proof of Lemma 19. Lemma 19 is in fact an oracle inequality. Equation (10) shows that the empirical error of the empirical risk minimizerf m can be decomposed into two parts: one is the approximation error inf f∈Σ sm  ∥f−f ρ∥2 L2(dµn) +λ∥f∥ P1,s  , and the other is the complexity term 64δ 2 n of the model class. These two terms can be controlled respectively by the approximation theorem 5 and tools from statistical learning theory. Equation (10) also provides an upper bound for theℓ 1 path norm. As an example, suppose that Yhas a uniform boundB >0 (in which caseηis bounded and hence sub-Gaussian). In general, takingf= 0 in the basic inequality yields ∥fm∥P1,s ≤ 1 mλ mX i=1 y2 i ≤ B2 λ , but this estimate is quite crude. In fact, if we define f ∗ = arg min f∈Σ sn ∥f−f ρ∥2 L2(dµ) +λ∥f∥ P1,s , then by definition, ∥f ∗∥P1,s ≤ inf f∈Σ sn  ∥f−f ρ∥2 L2(dµ) +λ∥f∥ P1,s  λ . Sincef m is an approximation off ∗, we expect∥f m∥P1,s to admit a similar upper bound, and this upper bound is much smaller than B2 λ . Indeed, ifλis chosen appropriately (for example, so that limm→∞ λ(m) = 0), then inf f∈Σ s n  ∥f−f ρ∥2 L2(dµ) +λ∥f∥ P1,s  →0 whileB 2 is a fixed constant. This phenomenon has been studied in (Steinwart and Christmann, 2008; Shi et al., 2011, 2019), where iterative methods were proposed to improve this upper bound. However, using the oracle inequality above, we can directly obtain a better upper bound. Specifically, if we takeλ= 8δ2 n M , then with high probability ∥fm∥P1,s ≲ inf f∈Σ sm ∥f−f ρ∥2 L∞(Ω) λ +M , which is already very close to inf f∈Σ sn  ∥f−f ρ∥2 L2 (dµ)+λ∥f∥ P1,s  λ . Step 2: Localization and the uniform law of large numbers In the previous subsection, we treated the sample{(x i, yi)}n i=1 as fixed and obtained an upper bound on the empirical error. However, what we are actually concerned with is the generalization error. In this subsection, we regard the sample as random and use the localization technique and uniform law of large numbers introduced in (Wainwright, 2019, Chapter 14) to analyze the upper bound on the generalization error. Since thex i are independent and identically distributed, for any square-integrablefwe have E[∥f∥ 2 L2(dµn)] =E " 1 n nX i=1 f(x i)2 # =E[f(x) 2] =∥f∥ 2 L2(dµ) . Therefore, by the law of large numbers, under suitable conditions∥f∥ L2(dµn) converges to∥f∥ L2(dµ). For example, iffis bounded, then Hoeffding’s inequality (Wainwright, 2019, Proposition 2.5) yields P h ∥f∥ 2 L2(dµn) − ∥f∥2 L2(dµ) ≥t i ≤2e −cnt2 ,∀t >0. 35

Using the uniform law of large numbers, one can make this tail bound hold uniformly over func- tion classes satisfying certain conditions. This completes the proof of Theorem 8. Proofs of Theorems 11 and 12 To estimate the minimax lower bound, we first need some preliminaries. For each probability distributionρon Ω×R, we can define a regression function fρ(x) = Z R ydρ(y|x). Under our assumptions, we consider only thoseρsuch thatf ρ ∈W α,∞(Ω), and denote the collection of all suchρbyP. LetF={f ρ :ρ∈ P} ⊂W α,∞(Ω). We say that{f 1,· · ·, fT } ⊂ Fis a 2δ- separated setif for anyi̸=j, ∥fi −f j∥L2(dµ) ≥2δ . Writef i =f ρi , whereρ i ∈ P. We consider thehypothesis testing problemgenerated by the family of distributions{ρ 1,· · ·, ρT }: 1. Select an indexJ=juniformly at random from the index set [T] ={1,· · ·, T}. 2. GivenJ=j, draw a random sampleZfromρ j, that is, (Z|J=j)∼ρ j. Let ¯ρdenote the joint distribution of (Z, J). Note that the marginal distribution ofZis ¯ρ Z = 1 T PT j=1 ρj. Given a sampleZ, the hypothesis testing problem asks us to determine the random indexJfrom this sample. Atest functionis a mapping Ψ :Z →[T], and its error probability is defined by ¯ρ(Ψ(Z)̸=J). The hypothesis testing problem is closely related to the minimax lower bound, namely (Wainwright, 2019, Proposition 15.1), inf ˆf sup fρ∈F E h ∥ ˆf−f ρ∥2 L2(dµ) i ≥δ 2 inf Ψ ¯ρ(Ψ(Z)̸=J), where the infimum on the right-hand side is taken over all test functions. The difficulty of the hypothesis testing problem depends on the dependence betweenZandJ. In the most extreme case, ZandJare completely independent, in which case the observed sampleZis useless for determining J. To measure the dependence between two probability measures, we introduce some information- theoretic concepts. LetP,Qbe probability measures on a measurable spaceXwith base measureν. Since they always admit densities with respect to the base measureν= 1 2 (P+Q), we may assume without loss of generality that their density functions arep, q, respectively. The commonly used Kullback–Leibler divergencein information theory is defined by D(P∥Q) = Z X p(x) log p(x) q(x) dν(x), which can to some extent measure the distance between two probability measures, although it is not a metric, since one can easily verify that it does not satisfy symmetry:D(P∥Q)̸=D(Q∥P). We know that two random variables (Z, J) are independent if and only if their joint distribution ¯ρequals 38

the product of their marginals ¯ρZ ⊗¯ρJ . Thus, it is natural to quantify the dependence betweenZ andJvia the Kullback–Leibler divergence. Define themutual informationof (Z, J) by I(Z;J) =D(¯ρ∥¯ρZ ⊗¯ρJ ). By the properties of the Kullback–Leibler divergence, we haveI(Z;J)≥0, andI(Z;J) = 0 if and only ifZandJare independent. Lemma 21 (F ano, (W ainwright, 2019, Proposition 15.12))The minimax risk of the func- tion classFsatisfies inf ˆf sup fρ∈F E h ∥ ˆf−f ρ∥2 L2(dµ) i ≥δ 2  1− I(Z;J) + log 2 logT  . By Fano’s lemma, it suffices to find an upper bound onI(Z;J) in order to obtain a minimax lower bound. For this purpose, we can use the Yang–Barron inequality. Lemma 22 (Y ang–Barron, Y ang and Barron (1999))LetN KL (ε;P)be theε-covering num- ber ofPunder the square-root KL divergence. Then I(Z;J)≤inf ε>0   ε2 + logN KL (ε;P) . Combining Fano’s lemma and the Yang–Barron inequality, we can derive the minimax lower bound in two steps: Proof of Theorem 11.LetBW α,∞(Ω) denote the unit ball inW α,∞(Ω). Whenµ∼U(Ω), we have logN(δ, BWα,∞(Ω),∥ · ∥L2(dµ))≍logN(δ, BW α,∞(Ω),∥ · ∥L2(Ω))≍δ − d α . Here the second equivalence follows from the Birman–Solomjak theorem (Birman and Solomyak, 1967). Therefore, we may choose aδ-separated set{f 1,· · ·, fT }inW α,∞(Ω) such that logT≳δ − d α . Forj∈[T], letρ j denote the distribution of the random vectoryconditional on{x i}n i=1 when the true function isf j, namely, yi =f j(xi) +η i, η i ∼ N(0, σ2), y= (y 1,· · ·, yn)⊤ . Letµ n denote the joint distribution of{x i}n i=1. Then the joint distribution of (y,{x i}n i=1) isρ j ⊗µ n. By the formula for the Kullback–Leibler divergence between Gaussian measures, for anyj̸=k, D(ρj ⊗µ n∥ρk ⊗µ n) =E x[D(ρj∥ρk)] = n 2σ2 ∥fj −f k∥2 L2(dµ) . Therefore, logN KL (ε;P)≤logN ε r 2σ2 n , BWα,∞(Ω),∥ · ∥L2(dµ) ! ≲  ε2 n − d 2α . Combining Fano’s lemma and the Yang–Barron inequality, we can prove the conclusion by exactly the same argument as in the previous theorem. This completes the proof of Theorem 11. The proof of Theorem 12 follows by exactly the same method, and is therefore omitted. 39

Canonical Regularisation of Wide Feature-Learning Neural Networks George Whittle†, Pranav Vaidhyanathan, Juliusz Ziomek, Natalia Ares, Maike A. Osborne Department of Engineering Science University of Oxford † Corresponding Author:george.whittle@reuben.ox.ac.uk Abstract Wide neural networks in the feature-learning regime drive modern deep learning, and yet they remain far less studied than their kernel-regime counterparts. We consider a critical yet under-explored difference between these two regimes: the regulariser and prior implied by gradient flow training. Thiscanonical regularisa- tionproperty is well-studied in kernel regime networks — of all the infinite global minima, gradient flow selects exactly the vanishing ridge solution — and underpins the celebrated NN-GP correspondence, precisely allowing the modelling of noise during training. However, we prove ridge regularisation biases gradient flow in feature-learning regime networks, even in the infinitesimal limit of vanishing regu- larisation. Over training, ridge distorts the inductive bias of the network, with a particular damage done to pretrained networks where the implicit prior is informa- tive. We resolve this by axiomatising the canonical regulariser as a regime-agnostic function-space energy and lift, which uniquely identifies ridge in the kernel regime, and crucially generalises to the feature-learning regime. By studying the Rieman- nian geometry of feature-learning networks, we derivegeodesic ridgefrom our framework, generalising ridge to the feature-learning regime. Correspondingly, we prove the canonical function-space prior is aRiemannian Gibbs Process, generalis- ing the more familiar Gaussian Process. As a practical contribution, we proposearc ridgeas a minimax-robust, scalable surrogate to geodesic ridge, revealing a deep relationship between early stopping and canonical regularisation across learning regimes. Finally, we demonstrate the consequences of our theory empirically on both image processing and NLP transfer-learning problems. 1 Introduction Wide neural networks now drive nearly every advance in modern deep learning, yet our theoretical understanding of these networks operating in the regime in which they actuallylearn features—rather than evolving as effectively fixed kernel machines—remains strikingly limited. The kernel regime, formalised by the Neural Tangent Kernel (NTK) of Jacot et al. [17] and elaborated by Lee et al. [25] and Du et al. [10], supports a complete account of training dynamics, generalisation, and Bayesian inference; the feature-learning regime supports no comparable toolkit. This asymmetry would be of mainly theoretical interest were it not that many practices inherited from the kernel-regime continue to be applied in the feature-learning regime by default, despite the foundations on which they rest no longer holding. This paper confronts one such case—which regulariser, and which function-space prior, gradient-flow training implicitly favours—and identifies the geometric reason. The kernel-regime picture is well understood. An overparametrised model has an entire continuum of zero-loss interpolators, yet gradient flow selects a single, well-defined point from this continuum: the unique vanishing-regularisation limit ofanchored ridge, ∥θ−θ 0∥2, the squared parameter- space distance from initialisation [34, 17, 13, 33]. This identification runs deeper than coincidence: Preprint. arXiv:2605.18180v1 [stat.ML] 18 May 2026

The gradient flow trajectory (solid blue curve) lies on Mflow (θ0) and converges to θ⋆. The geodesic distance dflow (θ0,θ ⋆) on Mflow (θ0) (dashed blue curve) underpins the canonical regulariser. The regularisation gradients at θ⋆, ∇θd2 (θ,θ 0) (dashed) and ∇θL2(γtrain) (solid), are shown in orange; both lie in Tθ⋆ Mflow (θ0) along the realised gradient flow direction, and so do not bias gradient flow as standard and anchored ridge do.Right: kernel regime geometry. The flow manifold is an affine subspace—geodesics are straight lines, and the canonical regulariser reduces to anchored ridge. vanishing-regularisation alone is not sharp enough to pin out anchored ridge from its uncountably- many quadratic competitors (Proposition C.5); a pair of natural axioms on the function-space energy (Theorem C.4) is needed to single it out. Anchored ridge equals the squared Reproducing Kernel Hilbert Space (RKHS) norm of the change in network output under the NTK over training, so the same penalty governs both parameter and function space, and the corresponding implicit prior over functions is Gaussian—the celebrated Neural Network–Gaussian Process correspondence. From this single equivalence flow virtually all the analytical conveniences of kernel-regime networks: principled treatment of label noise, optimal shrinkage, calibrated uncertainty, predictable transfer behaviour. Anchored ridge is, in this sense, thecanonicalregulariser of the kernel regime. Standard ridge ∥θ∥2 [16, 41] recovers the same selection only when θ0 =0 ; for any non-zero initialisation it already biases the gradient-flow limitwithinthe kernel regime (Remark C.4, Figure 2, right). Outside the kernel regime, the same picture decomposes. Yang and Hu [49] establish a sharp dynamical dichotomy: in the regime in which networks actually learn features and transfer between tasks, the Jacobian evolves throughout training, the kernel depends on optimisation history, parameter trajectories leave any single affine plane, and the function-space identity underlying anchored ridge’s central role breaks. In this regime, formalised by the Tensor Programs framework [46, 47, 48, 49] and complemented by mean-field analyses [6, 29], no analogous canonical regulariser has been identified. Practitioners apply weight decay [16] or anchored ridge by default, even though the kernel-regime arguments justifying these choices no longer apply. The consequences are not abstract: weight decay is implicated in the grokking phenomenon [36, 44] and in a documented degradation of pretrained representations during fine-tuning [23, 24]. We argue that the gap is geometric. Behind every overparametrised network lie three natural parameter-space objects (Section 3.1): theflow orbit, the region of parameter space gradient flow can ever explore from a given initialisation; thegauge-fixed flow manifold, the canonicaln-dimensional surface within this orbit that contains the realised trajectory and stands in one-to-one correspondence with output values near the interpolator—in effect, a parameter-space chart of function space; and the output fibres, directions transverse to the flow manifold along which predictions on training points do not change, but along which

predictions at unseen inputs can change drastically. In the kernel regime these collapse: orbit, flow manifold, and the affine plane θ0 + Im(J⊤) coincide, the trajectory is confined to it, and the fibres meet it orthogonally everywhere. Anchored ridge, the squared chord from θ0 to θ, lives entirely within this plane (Figure 1, right); its gradient never deflects parameters along the fibres, so the gradient-flow limit is preserved as the regularisation strength shrinks. In the feature-learning regime the flow orbit can grow beyond n dimensions, gauge-fixing extracts a curved n-dimensional manifold (Figure 1, left), and the trajectory bends through this surface. Anchored ridge, still measured along Euclidean chords, no longer respects this curvature: at the gradient-flow limit its gradient acquires a component along the output fibres, pulling parameters along directions invisible to 2

the training loss but consequential at test points. The resulting bias is silent at the loss level, persists in the limit as regularisation shrinks to zero, and is what we prove formally (Theorem 3.1). The same geometry suggests the cure. The intrinsic counterpart of squared Euclidean distance on a curved manifold is squared geodesic distance, and the corresponding intrinsic counterpart of anchored ridge isgeodesic ridge(Proposition 4.1): squared geodesic distance from initialisation along the flow manifold, plus an analogous on-fibre term. Its gradient at the gradient-flow limit lies tangent to the flow manifold along the realised flow direction (Figure 1, left, dashed orange), and through the standard MAP correspondence it yields a function-space prior that generalises the NTK Gaussian Process to curved geometry: theRiemannian Gibbs Process. In the kernel regime the manifold flattens, geodesics straighten into chords, and both objects reduce to anchored ridge and the NTK GP. The progression standard ridge → anchored ridge → geodesic ridge thus captures a sequence of geometric corrections: one Euclidean for initialisation, then one Riemannian for manifold curvature. Geodesic distances are computationally inaccessible at scale. Our final contribution identifies a tractable upper bound that retains the alignment property:arc ridge, the squared total path length of the training trajectory itself. Arc ridge requires no manifold solvers and no second-order information; it is a single scalar accumulated over training updates. It nevertheless inherits the no-bias property of geodesic ridge along the realised gradient-flow direction, sandwiches geodesic ridge from above against an anchored-ridge lower bound, sits at the minimax-optimal point within this sandwich, and reduces formally to early stopping under exact gradient flow. The combination places it within reach of any standard training pipeline. Contributions. • A geometric framework for wide feature-learning networks.We characterise the parameter-space geometry of any overparametrised network satisfying a single uniform- conditioning assumption (Assumption 2.1) via the orbit / gauge-fixed flow manifold / output fibre triple of Section 2. The kernel regime is recovered as the affine special case in which all three coincide withθ 0 + Im(J⊤). • Both standard and anchored ridge provably bias gradient flow.Standard ridge fails in both regimes (Remark C.4, Figure 2, right): in the kernel regime by ignoring initialisation, in the feature-learning regime additionally through curvature. Anchored ridge—the canonical kernel-regime regulariser—fails in the feature-learning regime because manifold curvature deflects its gradient along output fibres invisible to the training loss, biasing the gradient-flow limit by an amount that does not vanish as regularisation shrinks (Theorem 3.1, Figure 2, left). This challenges the kernel-regime foundations on which much common regularisation practice rests. • A first-principles characterisation that uniquely identifies anchored ridge in the kernel regime and uniquely generalises it.We axiomatise the canonical function-space energy as the minimum control-theoretic energy required to transport network outputs from initialisa- tion to a target (Definition 4.1), and prove uniqueness in the kernel regime under invariance and orthogonal-additivity axioms (Theorem C.4), recovering anchored ridge and the NTK Gaussian Process exactly. The same construction lifts to parameter space (Definition 4.3) and extends to the feature-learning regime, identifyinggeodesic ridge(Proposition 4.1) as the canonical regulariser and theRiemannian Gibbs Processas

the canonical function-space prior, with the unbiased gradient-flow selection property preserved (Theorem 4.2). • Arc ridge: a scalable, minimax-robust surrogate.Squared training-path length upper- bounds geodesic ridge along Mflow (θ0) (Theorem 4.3), retains the no-bias property along the realised gradient-flow direction, sits at the minimax-optimal point of the sandwich between anchored ridge below and arc ridge above (Proposition F.2), and is formally equivalent to early stopping under exact gradient flow. Empirically (Section 5), arc ridge recovers the predicted advantages on transfer-learning settings where the implicit prior carries information. 2 Problem Setting We study overparametrised supervised models f(x;θ) :X ×Θ→ Y with scalar output Y ⊆R, trained on some dataset D= (X, Y)∈(X × Y) n with n≪m= dim Θ finite and distinct inputs Xi ̸=X j for i̸=j . The input space X is arbitrary. We assume f is expressible in the 3

Tensor Programs Framework[ 46, 47, 48, 49], and possesses only smooth non-linearities. We write g(θ) : Θ→ Y n for the stacked output vector with g(θ) i =f(X i;θ) . The model-output Jacobian is J(θ)∈R n×m with J(θ) ij = ∂g(θ) i ∂θj , and the instantaneous NTK Gram matrix is K(θ) =J(θ)J(θ) ⊤ ∈R n×n. We study supervised training under gradient flow dynamics with the canonical SSE loss L(g(θ), Y) =∥g(θ)−Y∥ 2, i.e. −J(θ) ⊤ ∇g(θ) L(g(θ), Y) =−2J(θ) ⊤ (g(θ)−Y) , corre- sponding to function-space dynamics ˙g(θ) =−2K(θ) (g(θ)−Y) . We denote the value of θ after running gradient flow from θ0 for a time t as ϕgf (θ0, t). We assume L is minimised by an interpolatingsolution, achieved by any θ⋆ ∈Θ ⋆ ={θ:g(θ) =Y} . Note further that extensions of our results to strictly convex, smooth losses are deferred to the appendix. Given the above setup, our results rest on only a single assumption: Assumption 2.1(Uniform Jacobian conditioning).There exist an open neighbourhood U⊃Θ ⋆ and constants0< c≤C <∞such that for allθ∈U, c≤σ min(J(θ))andσ max(J(θ))≤C. Furthermore, assume that for any given initialisationθ 0,ϕ gf (θ0, t)∈U∀t∈[0,∞). This is a standard assumption in wide-network analysis: all infinite-width kernel-regime literature assumes it, and it is justified in the feature-learning regime as under µP scaling for Tensor Programs networks with smooth activations [47, 48], σmin(J(θ 0)) concentrates around its positive infinite- width limit at initialisation, and continuity propagates the bound to a neighbourhood. Furthermore, we verify this empirically along flow trajectories in Appendix H. 1 3 Ridge Biases Gradient Flow in the Feature Learning Regime The first core theoretical contribution of this work is an exposition of the feature-learning regime as apathologyof both anchored and standard ridge. To understand this, we must first understand the geometryunderlying wide networks, and how it differs between the two regimes. 3.1 Parameter-space Geometry We begin by illuminating the parameter-space geometry of any overparametrised network satisfying Assumption 2.1, restricting ourselves to the region U for complete rigour. This geometry is best understood as a collection of lower-dimensional subsets, which collectively span all of U. The first of these is the horizontal orbit generated by the directions available to gradient flow: Definition 3.1(Flow orbit).Let Hθ := ImJ(θ) ⊤ ⊂T θΘ denote the horizontal distribution induced by the model-output Jacobian. Theflow orbitthrough θ0, denotedO flow (θ0)⊆Θ, is the reachable set of the horizontal control system ˙θ(t) =J(θ(t)) ⊤u(t),u∈L 2([0, T],Rn), namely Oflow (θ0) := n θ(T) :T >0,u∈L 2([0, T],Rn), ˙θ(t) =J(θ(t)) ⊤u(t),θ(0) =θ 0 o . The flow orbit is the fundamental control-theoretic object. In general it is a sub-Riemannian reachable set as its tangent directions are generated by the Lie closure of H, and therefore its dimension can exceed n. Thus the orbit itself should not be identified with function space. In order to move from function-space quantities back to parameter space, we fix a gauge, selecting a single representative from the flow orbit for each output value. This gives us our second fundamental geometric object: 1An immediate useful consequence of Assumption 2.1 is that gradient flow

converges to an interpolating solu- tion: θ⋆ := lim t→∞ ϕgf (θ0, t)∈Θ ⋆. Indeed, the loss is a valid Lyapunov function with time-derivative ˙L= −2 (g(θ)−Y) ⊤ K(θ) (g(θ)−Y) . Since J(θ) has full rank along the trajectory, K(θ) :=J(θ)J(θ) ⊤ ⪰c 2I≻0 , so ˙L <0wheneverg(θ)̸=Y. Convergence to someθ ⋆ ∈Θ ⋆ thus follows, in fact exponentially with rate at least2c 2. 4

Intuitively, Θ (c) represents the collection of vertical directions in parameter space which do not affect training predictions g(θ) , but can have a marked effect on the network’s predictions away from the training inputs X. These directions will be of particular interest to us, as any parameter motion here is invisible to the training loss, but can dramatically affect the generalisation error. Geometrically, these output fibres are embedded submanifolds on U of codimension n (that is, they are smooth surfaces of dimension m−n ) as c is defined as a regular value of g, satisfy Θ (Y) = Θ ⋆ ∩U , and have tangent spacekerJ(θ)so are everywhere orthogonal to horizontal gradient flow trajectories. 3.2 The failure of ridge in the feature-learning regime Equipped with our geometric understanding of parameter-space, exposing the failure of both standard and anchored ridge in the feature learning regime is straightforward. The feature-learning regime is characterised bycurvedgeometry — features evolve over training and J changes meaningfully with θ — thus Mflow (θ0) curves meaningfully between θ0 and θ⋆. As illustrated in Figure 1, this stands in stark contrast to the geometry of the kernel regime where J is constant on U and Mflow (θ0)≡ Oflow (θ0) =   θ0 +J ⊤u:u∈R n is an affine subspace. Now consider the interaction of these geometries with the anchored ridge regulariser, ∥θ−θ 0∥2. The contribution to gradient flow training of this regulariser is −2 (θ−θ 0), e.g. the chord from θ0 to θ. In the kernel regime, this always lies within the tangent space of Mflow (θ0), and thus anchored ridge can never induce parameter movement off-manifold — it iscompatiblewith gradient flow (Proposition C.10). This geometric alignment is precisely what causes the vanishing regularisation property underpinning the NN-GP correspondence, and as shown by Ordoñez et al. [33], allows us to explicitly model observation noise in the training set through an MAP interpretation of training. In the feature-learning regime, however, curvature ofMflow (θ) causes this gradient to generically have a component lying in off-manifold fibre directions. The implications of this are significant: the regularisation gradient causes parameter displacements invisible to the loss function and thus uncontrollable by the training set, but which can drastically change behaviour away from the training set by destroying the network’s covariance structure and inductive bias imbued by initialisation. We can formally describe this pathology by its bias to gradient flow in the infinitesimal limit: Theorem 3.1.Consider a wide feature-learning regime network satisfying Assumption 2.1. Let θ⋆ λ∥∆θ∥2 := arg min θ∈Θ L(g(θ), Y) +λ∥θ−θ 0∥2. Then θ⋆ ∥∆θ∥2 := lim λ↓0 θ⋆ λ∥∆θ∥2 ̸=θ ⋆, whereθ ⋆ := lim t→∞ ϕgf (θ0, t)is the time-limit of unregularised gradient flow. An identical argument proves failure of standard ridge in both kernelandfeature-learning regimes. Figure 2 illustrates this pathology exactly. Intuitively, the limiting dynamics has two timescales. On the fast timescale, small- λ regularised trajectories stay close to the unregularised one, seemingly converging to θ⋆. However, any λ >0 gives rise to a slow timescale on which the uncontrolled regularisation gradient induces drift fibre directions, biasing the time-limit.

For larger λ — necessary in problems with little data or noisy targets — this pathology becomes immediate, yielding significant divergence from the unregularised trajectory. While not always a bad thing in practice—this effect is precisely what causes weight decay-induced grokking[ 36, 44] and underpins the adaptive kernel representer theorem of Lauditi et al. [24]—in cases where the feature geometry is well-suited to the task a priori (i.e. pretrained networks), it can harm generalisation by replacing useful inductive bias with drifted features purely fitting noise [23]. We expose this pathology empirically in Section 5, which confirms a massive degradation of test performance under both standard and anchored ridge at high regularisation strengths. 4 Canonical Regularisation in the Feature-learning Regime Despite its pathology in the feature-learning regime, it is clear that anchored ridge’s harmony with gradient flow in the kernel regime is quite special. Indeed, this result implicitly underpins the NN-GP correspondence and Neural Tangent Kernel (NTK) theory as a whole [17]. It is thus reasonable to describe anchored ridge as thecanonical regulariserof kernel-regime networks, corresponding to 6

thefunction-space canonical priorNTK Gaussian Process. We thus set out to derive an equivalent regulariser for the feature-learning regime. Such a task requires us to return to regime-agnostic first-principles and axioms, ensuring unique identification of anchored ridge in the kernel regime as a validation of our framework. We propose a horizontal function-space energy formulation (with respect to the local Fisher metric K(θ)) as a suitably fundamental object underpinning such networks in the context of gradient flow training: Definition 4.1(Canonical function-space energy, prior, and regulariser).For some overparametrised network initialised at θ0, thecanonical function-space energyat c∈ Yn is the minimum control- theoretic energy required to transportg(θ)fromg(θ 0)tocunder gradient flow dynamics: E(c) := min u(t) Z 1 0 u(t)⊤K(t)u(t)dt subject to ˙θ=J(θ(t)) ⊤ u, K(t) =J(θ(t))J(θ(t)) ⊤ ,θ(0) =θ 0,θ(1) =g| −1 θ0 (c). For kernel-regime networks, the minimum energy control is the constant u⋆(t) =K −1 (c−g(θ 0)), recovering the standard squared RKHS norm E(c) = (c−g(θ 0))⊤ K −1 (c−g(θ 0))≡ ∥c−g(θ 0)∥2 RKHS (Proposition C.10(c)). The canonical function-space prior arises fromE(c)straightforwardly as a Gibbs distribution: Definition 4.2(Canonical function-space prior).The canonical function-space prior induced by E is aRiemannian Gibbs Process, with prediction density on the training set p(c|X)∝exp(−βE(c)). The temperature β is the sole degree of freedom under E (due to scale invariance of gradient flow), and thus takes the role of a global prior scale. Applying this Riemannian Gibbs Process prior to kernel-regime networks yields the familiar NTK GP, i.e. GP � f(·;θ 0),(2β) −1kNTK(·,· ′)  , with p(c|X)∝exp  −β∥c−g(θ 0)∥2 RKHS)  (Corol- lary C.3). Kolmogorov consistency over arbitrary finite test inputs at the fixed training set follows from a parametric pushforward (Proposition C.15); consistencyacrosstraining subsets remains an open problem (Remark C.5). Completing our generalisation requires one final task: a unique lift of the low-dimensional function- space energy to high-dimensional parameter-space. Reasonable axioms underpin this lift. Namely, we require the lift to be a) additive to the function-space energy E(c) associated with the training predictions g(θ) =c at θ (thereby forcing the lift to geometrically correspond only to Θ (c), b) quadratic in the ambient Euclidean metric of parameter-space, and c) uniformly 0 at the gauge-fixed representativeg| −1 θ0 (c). This uniquely gives us thecanonical lift(Theorem C.6): Definition 4.3(Canonical lift).Thecanonical liftofE(g(θ))to parameter-space is R(θ) :=E(g(θ)) + inf ψ 1Z 0 ˙ψ(t) 2 dt, where the infimum is over piecewise-C 1 paths ψ: [0,1]→Θ (g(θ))⊂Θ with ψ(0) =g| −1 θ0 (g(θ)) , ψ(1) =θ, andg(ψ(t)) =g(θ)∀t∈[0,1]. Verifying this lift in the kernel regime uniquely points to anchored ridge: We have thatE(g(θ)) = ∥c−g(θ 0)∥2 RKHS ≡ ∥∆θflow∥2 where ∆θflow is the projection ofθ−θ0 onto Mflow (θ0). Similarly, the lifted term is exactly ∥∆θfibre∥2, where ∆θfibre is the projection of θ−θ 0 onto Θ (g(θ)) . As in the kernel regime these two sets are orthogonal and affine, the Pythagorean identity yields R(θ) =∥∆θ flow∥2 +∥∆θ fibre∥2 =∥θ−θ 0∥2, uniquely recovering anchored ridge (Corollary C.2; Proposition C.10(d)). Framework in hand, we now present the canonical regulariser of the feature-learning regime: Proposition 4.1(Geodesic ridge).The canonical regulariser of feature-learning regime networks is geodesic ridge,
defined as d2 (θ,θ 0) :=d 2 flow � g|−1 θ0 (g(θ)),θ 0  +d 2 fibre � θ, g|−1 θ0 (g(θ))  , 7

where d2 flow � g|−1 θ0 (g(θ)),θ 0  ≡E(g(θ)) is the minimum horizontal distance in parameter-space from θ0 to the the gauge-fixed representativeg|−1 θ0 (g(θ)) , and d2 fibre � θ, g|−1 θ0 (g(θ))  is the geodesic distance from the gauge-fixed representative toθalong the output fibreΘ (g(θ)). While generally irreducible beyond this form, geodesic ridge is theoretically complete: Theorem 4.2(Canonical regulariser characterisation of geodesic ridge).Under Assumption 2.1: (a) Unique minimiser on each fibre.For every c∈C 0(θ0), g|−1 θ0 (c) is the unique minimiser ofd 2 (·,θ 0)overΘ (c)∩V. (b) Trajectory-level minimality.Once the gradient flow trajectory θ(t) enters V , θ(t) = θ′(g(θ(t))) and d2 (θ(t),θ 0) =E(g(θ(t))) . In particular every intermediate trajectory point is the minimum-cost representative of its output vector. (c)Unbiased limit property.lim λ↓0 L(g(θ), Y) +λd 2 (θ,θ 0) =θ ⋆. These properties are exemplified in Figure 2. We have thus generalised anchored ridge to the feature- learning regime, theoretically alleviating all issues associated with anchored ridge and thus allowing for principled modelling of observation noise and uncertainty in the feature-learning regime. 4.1 Arc Ridge: A Minimax-Robust Upper Bound to Geodesic Ridge Geodesic ridge, while theoretically complete, is intractable at scale. However, two observations combine to yield a practical surrogate. First, under exact gradient flow trajectories remain on Mflow (θ0) by construction, so the fibre term in Definition 4.1 vanishes. Second, geodesic ridge is upper-bounded by the squared length ofanyon-manifold path,including the realised training path: Definition 4.4(Squared path length regulariser (arc ridge)).For a training trajectory γ: [0, T]→R m, thesquared path length regulariser, orarc ridge, is L2(γ) := Z T 0 ∥˙γ(t)∥dt !2 . L2(γ)depends only on the image ofγas a subset ofΘ, not on its time-parametrisation. Theorem 4.3(Squared path length upper-bounds the canonical regulariser).Let γtrain : [0, T]→ Mflow (θ0)be any piecewise-C 1 path withγ train(0) =θ 0 andγ train(T) =θ. Then d2 flow (θ0,θ)≤L 2(γtrain); under exact gradient flow (where the fibre term vanishes),d2 (θ,θ 0)≤L 2(γtrain). The endpoint gradi- ent ∇θL2 lies along ˙γtrain(T −)∈Im(J(θ) ⊤)∩T θMflow (θ0) (in particular ∇θL2 ∈kerN(g(θ)) ), so regularisation by L2 introduces no component along the output fibres or indeed orthogonal to the realised gradient flow direction. Proof deferred to the appendix. The bound follows from the definition of geodesic distance as an infimum of arc lengths chained with the energy–length identity (Proposition C.9); proof deferred to the appendix. Anchored ridge trivially lower-bounds geodesic ridge on Mflow (θ0) as the solution to a relaxation of the same defining problem (Proposition C.13); the resulting sandwich and the shared vanishing-regularisation limit of all three regularisers atθ ⋆ are recorded in Corollary C.4. In the absence of tractability of the canonical regulariser, it is principled to instead solve a minimax- robust relaxation of MAP training, choosing the regulariser that minimises worst-case regret within the sandwich ∥θ−θ 0∥2 ≤d 2 (θ,θ 0)≤L 2(γtrain). This directly motivates arc ridge regularisation. Furthermore, arc ridge requires storing only the accumulated arc length—a single float—since the regularisation gradient direction is fully determined by the current data gradient. It is therefore applicable at scale, and is used

for most of our empirical results. Arc ridge has a further theoretical property: it connects to early stopping, since regularisation by accumulated path length behaves like a time-based stopping criterion and, given an estimate of observation noise, obviates the need for a validation set (Proposition F.4). The relationship between ridge regularisation and early stopping is well-studied, and in agreement with our theory, early stopping generalises trivially to feature-learning regime networks [11, 50], unlike anchored ridge. 8

At high λ, standard and anchored ridge degrade sharply as their off-manifold gradient components destroy the pretrained prior; arc ridge degrades only mildly, consistent with benign undertraining. At low λ, early stopping masks the bias and all regularisers converge — corroborating the formal equivalence between arc ridge and early stopping under gradient flow (Proposition F.4). 5 Empirical Consequences We first demonstrate the consequences of Theorems 3.1 and 4.2 in Figure 2, which displays gradient flow trajectories on the minimal example for each regime. Figure 3 then demonstrates the practical implications of our theory on realistic transfer-learning problems requiring feature-learning: Age regression on the UTKFace datatset [53] transferring from ResNet18 [14], and review score regression from the Yelp Review dataset [52] transferring from DistilBERT [39]. We present results for each regulariser across a range of different regularisation strengths, and combine with early stopping for maximum realism. To demonstrate the necessity of regularisation, we also include the ”Ridgeless“ (unregularised) performance (with no early stopping) as a baseline. Our theory predicts that at high regularisation strengths, the pathological gradient component of standard and anchored ridge will cause significant degradation of the prior imbued by pretraining, manifesting as very poor test performance. Conversely, arc ridge should only lose test performance in over-regularised regimes commensurately with undertraining — a much more controlled phenomenon — and thus exhibit a milder degradation. In contrast, at lower regularisation strengths the pathological components of anchored and standard ridge are dominated by early stopping — equivalent to optimally-tuned arc ridge — and thus all regularisers should converge in performance. This is exactly what we observe. 6 Conclusion and Limitations We proved that the implicit prior of a wide network is governed not by any norm on parameters, but by the geometry of the trajectories gradient flow can trace. We showed that while in the kernel regime anchored ridge is geometrically aligned with training, in the feature-learning regime training geometry curves, causing the regularisation gradient of anchored (and standard) ridge to pathologically degrade the implicit prior at initialisation — a critical failure mode in transfer learning, for example. We proposed a correction

to this by identifying two axioms uniquely identifying anchored in the kernel regime, and applied these to the curved geometry of feature-learning to obtain a principled framework not only yieldinggeodesic ridgeas a generalisation of anchored ridge to the feature-learning regime, but also revealing the implicit prior here as a Riemannian Gibbs process. For practical unbiased regularisation at scale, we proposedarc ridgea minimax-robust surrogate to true geodesic ridge, which is cheap to compute at scale and reveals deep links to early stopping. Finally, we validated the predictions empirically: regularisers that respect the geometry of training preserve the implicit prior; those that ignore it do not. Several directions remain open. Empirically, tighter scalable approximations to geodesic ridge, broader validation across architectures and tasks, and rigorous verification of Assumption 2.1 in concrete settings are immediate next steps. Theoretically, full Kolmogorov consistency of the Riemannian Gibbs Process across training subsets, analysis of the flow manifold in the µP infinite- width limit, and extensions to non-interpolating regimes [40, 27] and preconditioned optimisers such as Adam [22] or Muon [19] all remain open. 9
