A Assumptions We collect here the full list ofatomichypotheses on which the main results rely. Each entry states the assumption, lists the main-text results that depend on it, and sketches its justification. Smoothness of the flow manifold near the interpolant, normal hyperbolicity of Θ⋆, local properness of g|Mflow(θ0), and existence of a unique gradient flow ω-limit are derivable consequences — stated and proved as theorems or lemmas in Appendices D and E rather than assumed. (A.1) Smoothness.Statement.The network f(·;θ) is C ∞ in θ on the parameter domain of interest. Used by.All main-text results.Justification.Finite composition of smooth activations is C ∞ by elementary calculus. The practically relevant restriction is that activations satisfy the pseudo-Lipschitz / polynomially bounded growth hypothesis imposed by the TensorPrograms framework [ 47, 48], covering analytic variants oftanh, smoothSiLU, and smooth approximations ofReLU. (A.2) Data.Statement.The training dataset D= (X, Y) has finite n and distinct inputs Xi ̸=X j for i̸=j .Used by.All main-text results.Justification.Distinct inputs are necessary for the output fibre Θ (c) to be well-defined at every achievable c; finiteness of n ensures the Jacobian J(θ)∈R n×m has finite domain. (A.3) Loss and gradient flow dynamics.Statement.Training is the continuous-time gradient flow ˙θ=−∇ θL(g(θ), Y) =J(θ) ⊤ u with u:=−∂ cL(c, Y)| c=g(θ). The main text takes L(c, Y) =∥c−Y∥ 2 (squared error), giving u= 2(Y−g(θ)) , ∇2 cL(·, Y) = 2I , and Hessian ∇2 θL(g(θ), Y) = 2J(θ) ⊤ J(θ) + 2 P i(g(θ)−Y) i ∇2gi, which reduces to 2J(θ) ⊤ J(θ) at any θ∈Θ ⋆.Used by.All main-text results.Justification.A definitional choice of optimiser. Discrete- time gradient descent approximates this dynamics in the small step-size limit. The squared-error specialisation is natural for the canonical regression setting underlying the NTK–GP correspondence; extension to any strictly convex, µ-strongly-convex, M-smooth loss is straightforward (the SSE- specific identity Hessian becomes µI⪯ ∇ 2 cL(, Y)⪯M I everywhere needed) and is treated implicitly throughout the appendix. (A.4) Uniform Jacobian conditioning near interpolation.Statement.There exist an open neigh- bourhoodU⊇Θ ⋆ and constants0< c≤C <∞such that for allθ∈U, c≤σ min(J(θ))andσ max(J(θ))≤C. The gradient flow trajectory from θ0 converges to a limit point θ⋆ ∈Θ ⋆ ∩U (formally As- sumption 2.1).Used by.All main-text Theorems and Definitions in Sections 3 and 4; Lem- mas D.5, D.4, D.6, D.7; and proofs throughout Appendix E.Justification.A.4 is a single hypothesis collecting the geometric structure required for the local-NHIM analysis. Its three components — lower spectral bound on J(θ) , upper spectral bound, and convergence of the gradient flow trajectory to Θ⋆ — are all standard in wide-network analysis. UnderµP scaling for TensorPrograms-compatible architectures with smooth activations [47, 48], σmin(J(θ 0)) and σmax(J(θ 0)) concentrate around positive bounded infinite-width limits at initialisation; continuity propagates these bounds to a neigh- bourhood. Convergence of gradient flow to an interpolator is observed empirically and proved in special cases [17, 10]. Three downstream properties are derived (not assumed) from A.4: Θ⋆ ∩U is an embedded C ∞ codimension-n submanifold (regular-value theorem applied to g [26]); the loss Hessian ∇2L= 2J ⊤J on Θ⋆ ∩U has n eigenvalues bounded below by 2c2 and m−n zero eigen-

values with eigenspace kerJ(θ) , making Θ⋆ ∩U normally hyperbolic for the gradient flow vector field; and the leaf Mflow (θ0)∩V of the resulting stable-manifold foliation, restricted to a smaller neighbourhood V⊆U , is an embedded n-dimensional C ∞ submanifold tangent to Im(J(θ ⋆)⊤) at θ⋆ (Theorem C.2). RemarkA.1 ( µP scaling and Assumption 2.1).Under µP scaling for TensorPrograms-compatible architectures with smooth activations [ 47, 48], the NTK Gram matrix K(θ 0) =J(θ 0)J(θ 0)⊤ concentrates around its positive-definite infinite-width limit at initialisation, with eigenvalues bounded above and below at width m→ ∞. Continuity of σmin, σmax in θ then propagates the spectral bounds to a neighbourhood of θ0, and stability arguments along the gradient flow trajectory extend them to a neighbourhood ofΘ ⋆. 13

B Extended Related Work Implicit bias and minimum-norm interpolation.The connection between gradient flow and minimum-norm solutions in overparametrised linear models is classical [3]: the gradient flow limit is the Moore–Penrose pseudoinverse solution, which is also the ℓ2-minimum-norm interpolator. The same identity holds in kernel regression and in NTK-regime neural networks [17, 25, 10]: the gradient flow limit is the minimum-RKHS-norm interpolator, whose Gibbs distribution is the Gaussian Process prior N(g(θ 0), K)[37, 13, 33]. Thekernel vs. richdichotomy of Woodworth et al. [43] delineates when networks behave like linear models (kernel regime) vs. when feature learning occurs. Our contribution is to identify theenergyunderlying this selection—not merely the norm—and to generalise it to the feature-learning regime. Theorem 3.1 shows that, beyond the kernel regime, the min-Euclidean-norm interpolator and the gradient flow limit diverge; the energy-based view moves beyond the implicit-bias framing to a positive characterisation of the canonical function-space prior. Implicit bias in classification and structured models.The implicit bias of gradient descent has been characterised in several other settings. For classification with the logistic loss on linearly separable data, gradient descent converges in direction to the max-margin (hard-SVM) solution [40, 18]; for homogeneous networks this extends to a KKT-based characterisation of the margin [27]. For matrix factorisation, gradient descent induces an implicit nuclear-norm penalty [12], and for wide two-layer networks trained with the logistic loss a related max-margin structure emerges [7]. These results characterise thedirectionof implicit bias in specific architectures or losses; our framework instead characterises thefunction-space energyunderlying gradient flow selection and provides a geometric generalisation that applies across architectures and losses within the feature-learning regime. Feature-learning theory.The mean-field limit [ 6, 29, 38] describes the infinite-particle dynamics of two-layer networks in a regime where the Jacobian varies substantially, complementing the NTK picture. The Tensor Programs framework of Yang and Hu [49] provides a unified characterisation of wide networks via a dynamical dichotomy between kernel and feature-learning regimes, with µP scaling [45] as the canonical parameterisation enabling stable feature learning. Recent work has analysed what gradient descent actuallylearnsin the feature-learning regime: Damian et al. [8] prove that gradient descent can learn representations unavailable to fixed-kernel methods, and Lauditi et al. [24] characterise the adaptive kernel that emerges at the feature-learning infinite-width limit. Our work complements these results by characterising theregulariserthat is canonical for the dynamics induced by gradient flow in this regime, rather than describing what those dynamics converge to. Weight decay beyond RKHS regularisation.Weight decay has been studied as a driver of phenomena beyond its interpretation as an RKHS prior. The grokking phenomenon—delayed generalisation long after data loss reaches zero—was first documented by Power et al. [36] and has been given a provable analysis in the setting of ridge regression by Xu et al. [44]. The effect of weight decay on the features learned by neural networks, and its connection to the adaptive kernel at the feature-learning limit, is analysed by Lauditi et al. [24]. Theorem 3.1 and Remark C.4 provide a complementary theoretical perspective: in the feature-learning regime, both standard ridge and anchored ridge select interpolating solutions that differ systematically from the

gradient flow limit. Their practical effect is thereforesolution-set modificationrather thanprior specification, and the benefits observed in practice may arise partly from this solution-selection property rather than from Bayesian regularisation. Geometry-aware optimisers.Natural gradient descent [ 2] preconditions the gradient by the inverse Fisher information matrix, which in the supervised learning setting equals K(θ) −1 [28, 32]. Under this preconditioning, function-space dynamics become isotropic and training trajectories approximate geodesics on Mflow (θ0) [51, 5]; several wide-network approximations reproduce this isotropic behaviour [20]. Recent optimisers implement related principles at scale [ 19, 4]. Our work is complementary: rather than proposing a new optimiser, we characterise theregularisercanonical for the geometry induced by gradient flow; preconditioning the gradient flow by the natural-gradient (NTK) metric is a direct route to tightening our path-length bound, but we leave its full development to future work. 14

C Extended Results We collect here the formal statements of results whose informal versions appear in the main text. Proofs of all results appear in Appendix E. C.1 Convergence of gradient flow under uniform Jacobian conditioning Proposition C.1(Gradient-flow convergence to an interpolator).Under Assumptions A.1, A.3, and 2.1, the gradient flow trajectory θ(t) =ϕ gf (θ0, t)converges to a limit θ⋆ ∈Θ ⋆ at exponential rate. For squared-error loss, ∥g(θ(t))−Y∥ ≤ ∥g(θ0)−Y∥e −2c2t, where c is the lower spectral bound of Assumption 2.1. In particular,θ(t)stays inUfor allt≥0, andω(θ 0) := limt→∞ θ(t)exists. C.2 Local manifold and metric structure ofM flow (θ) Theorem C.2(Local manifold chart).Under Assumption 2.1, there exists an open neighbourhood V⊆Uofθ ⋆ such that: (i)Θ ⋆ ∩V is an embedded C ∞ codimension-n submanifold of V with Tθ(Θ⋆ ∩V) = kerJ(θ) at everyθ∈Θ ⋆ ∩V; (ii) the gauge-fixed flow manifold Mflow (θ0)∩V is an embedded C ∞ n-submanifold of V tangent atθ ⋆ toIm(J(θ ⋆)⊤), varyingC ∞-smoothly with the basepoint acrossΘ ⋆ ∩V; (iii) the restriction g|Mflow(θ0)∩V is a C ∞ diffeomorphism onto its open image C0(θ0) := g(Mflow (θ0)∩V)⊆R n, with inverse the gauge-fixed representativeg| −1 θ0 (c). Theorem C.3(Metric gap of Mflow (θ0) from the kernel-regime affine plane).Let A(c) := Dg|−1 θ0 (c) and write A(c) =A ∥(c) +N(c) with A∥(c) :=J � g|−1 θ0 (c) ⊤ K � g|−1 θ0 (c) −1 and N(c) := (I−P g|−1 θ0 (c))A(c) , where Pθ is the orthogonal projector onto Im(J(θ) ⊤). Then the pullback metricG(c) :=A(c) ⊤A(c)onC 0(θ0)admits the orthogonal decomposition G(c) =K � g|−1 θ0 (c) −1 +N(c) ⊤N(c), so G⪰K −1 pointwise, with equality G(c) =K � g|−1 θ0 (c) −1 if and only if Tg|−1 θ0 (c)Mflow (θ0) = Im(J � g|−1 θ0 (c) ⊤ ) (the kernel-regime alignment). Moreover, along the realised gradient flow tra- jectory θ(t) =g| −1 θ0 (c(t)), the velocity ˙c(t) satisfies N(c(t)) ˙c(t) = 0, so the trajectory’s G-energy reduces to ˙c⊤K(θ(t)) −1 ˙c. C.3 Uniqueness of the canonical kernel-regime energy and lift The kernel-regime canonical function-space energy E(c) = (c−g(θ 0))⊤K −1(c−g(θ 0)) of Definition 4.1 is the unique continuous output-space energy compatible with the natural geometry of the flow manifold; “naturality” alone — the property that vanishing-regularisation MAP estimation recovers the gradient flow limit — does not single it out. We make these statements formal here. Theorem C.4(Output-space energy uniqueness).Let G∈R n×n be symmetric positive definite. Supposeρ G :R n →[0,∞)is continuous, satisfiesρ G(0) = 0, is nontrivial, and obeys (i) (G−1-isometry invariance) ρG(Uc) =ρ G(c) for every U∈R n×n with U ⊤G−1U=G −1; (ii) (orthogonal additivity)u ⊤G−1v= 0 =⇒ρ G(u+v) =ρ G(u) +ρ G(v). Thenρ G(c) =αc ⊤G−1cfor some constantα >0. In words, axiom (i) asks that ρG depends only on the G−1-geometry of the displacement, not on the basis we describe it in (the analogue of rotational invariance for the standard inner product); axiom (ii) asks that G−1-orthogonal displacements contribute additively (the function-space analogue of the Pythagorean identity). Together they leave a single scale degree of freedom, absorbed into the prior temperature β. Applied with G=K −1, this

characterises the kernel-regime canonical energy of Definition 4.1 uniquely. The corresponding parameter-space lift inherits this uniqueness: in the kernel regime, the orthogonal decomposition Rm = Im(J ⊤)⊕kerJ coincides with the flow-tangent 15

/ fibre decomposition globally, and the squared ambient distance along Θ (g(θ)) is the unique fibre extension respecting orthogonal additivity in this decomposition. RemarkC.1 (Both axioms of Theorem C.4 are individually necessary).Dropping axiom (i) (isometry invariance), the functional ρG(c) := (c 1 −g(θ 0)1)2 is continuous, vanishes at the origin, is non- trivial, and is orthogonally additive (every basis-aligned splitting of c trivially satisfies the additivity hypothesis); but it depends on the chosen first coordinate and is not of the formαc ⊤G−1c. Dropping axiom (ii) (orthogonal additivity), the functional ρG(c) := � c⊤G−1c 2 is continuous, G−1-isometry invariant, and non-trivial; but G−1-orthogonal displacements satisfy ρG(u+v) = (s+t) 2 ̸= s2 +t 2 =ρ G(u) +ρ G(v) for s, t >0, so axiom (ii) fails. Hence both axioms are individually necessary for the uniqueness conclusion. Proposition C.5(No-go for naturality alone in the kernel regime).Let θ⋆ be the gradient-flow interpolator from θ0 in a kernel-regime model with constant JacobianJ∈R n×m and K=J J ⊤ ≻0 . Consider the two-parameter family of trajectory-blind quadratic regularisers F:= n Ra,B(θ) :=a∥θ−θ 0∥2 + � J(θ−θ 0) ⊤ B � J(θ−θ 0)  :a >0, B∈Sym + n o , where Sym+ n denotes the cone of symmetric positive semi-definite n×n matrices. Distinct (a, B) give distinct regularisers in F (their Hessians 2(aIm +J ⊤BJ) differ), and distinct elements of F correspond to distinct Gaussian MAP parameter priors N(θ 0, 1 2λ (aIm +J ⊤BJ) −1). Yet every element ofFattains the same vanishing-regularisation limit: lim λ↓0 arg min θ  L(g(θ), Y) +λR a,B(θ)  =θ ⋆ ∀(a, B)∈R >0 ×Sym + n . The vanishing-regularisation property (“naturality”) therefore doesnotpin out anchored ridge — only the special case B= 0 does, and singling it out within F requires the additional orthogonal- additivity axiom of Theorem C.4. Theorem C.6(Uniqueness of the canonical lift).Fix the canonical function-space energy E of Definition 4.1. Among all functionalsR: Θ→[0,∞)satisfying (a) (additivity to E) R(θ) =E(g(θ)) +S(θ) for some S: Θ→[0,∞) depending only on the on-fibre displacement ofθfromg| −1 θ0 (g(θ)); (b) (Euclidean fibre quadratic) the restriction of S to each fibre Θ (g(θ))∩V is a quadratic in the ambient Euclidean metric inherited fromR m; (c) (vanishing at the gauge-fixed representative)S(g| −1 θ0 (g(θ))) = 0, the unique solution (up to a positive scale) is R(θ) =E(g(θ)) + inf ψ Z 1 0 ˙ψ(t) 2 dt=E(g(θ)) +d 2 fibre � g|−1 θ0 (g(θ)),θ  , where the infimum runs over piecewise-C 1 paths ψ: [0,1]→Θ (g(θ)) with ψ(0) =g| −1 θ0 (g(θ)) , ψ(1) =θ . In the kernel regime this reduces to anchored ridge; in the feature-learning regime it is geodesic ridge of Proposition 4.1. Theorem C.7(Combined uniqueness of geodesic ridge as the canonical regulariser).Under As- sumption 2.1, the only functional R: Θ→[0,∞) on a neighbourhood of Θ⋆ which simultaneously satisfies the function-space-energy axioms of Theorem C.4 (with G=K −1 at θ0) on the output channel g(θ) , the lift axioms (a)–(c) of Theorem C.6 on the parameter-space channel, and the vanishing-regularisation property arg min(L(g(·), Y) +λR)→θ ⋆ as λ↓0 , is, up

to a single positive scale, R(θ) =d 2 (θ,θ 0) =d 2 flow � θ0, g|−1 θ0 (g(θ))  +d 2 fibre � g|−1 θ0 (g(θ)),θ  , i.e. geodesic ridge. In the kernel regime this collapses to anchored ridge; the corresponding function- space prior is uniquely the Riemannian Gibbs Process of Definition 4.2, collapsing to the NTK Gaussian process under linearJ. C.4 Geometry of the flow manifold and output fibres Proposition C.8(Output fibres are smooth submanifolds).Under Assumption 2.1, for every c∈g(U) the output fibreΘ (c)∩Uis an embeddedC ∞ submanifold ofR m of codimensionn. 16

RemarkC.2 (Path-connectivity of output fibres).In the overparametrised regime m≫n , the connected component of Θ (c)∩V containing g|−1 θ0 (c) is path-connected via the implicit-function- theorem chart on Θ (c) over V [26], so the fibre distance dfibre � θ, g|−1 θ0 (c)  = θ−g| −1 θ0 (c) in Definition 4.3 is finite. Corollary C.1(Unique fibre–flow intersection in V ).Under the hypotheses of Theorem C.2, for every c∈C 0(θ0) the intersection (Mflow (θ0)∩V)∩Θ (c) consists of exactly one point, which we denoteg| −1 θ0 (c). In particularθ ⋆ =θ ′(Y), and theω-limitω(θ 0) = limt→∞ ϕgf (θ0, t) =θ⋆. Proposition C.9(Constant-speed optimality).Among piecewise- C 1 paths γ: [0,1]→ (Mflow (θ0),ambient Euclidean) with fixed endpoints, the squared-energy R 1 0 ∥˙γ(t)∥2 dt is min- imised by constant-speed parametrisations of length-minimising paths, and at the minimiser the energy equals the squared arc length:E(γ opt) =L 2(γopt). RemarkC.3 (Existence and non-uniqueness of minimising geodesics).On the smooth Riemannian manifold (Mflow (θ0)∩V, gambient) near θ⋆, geodesic completeness on a compact sublevel set of the squared distance from θ0 follows from local properness of g|Mflow(θ0)∩V (Lemma D.7); the Hopf–Rinow theorem [9, Ch. 7, Thm. 2.8] then yields a length-minimising geodesic between any two endpoints in the same component. Minimisers need not be unique: conjugate points and non- trivial topology of Mflow (θ0)∩V can create multiple equal-length shortest paths. The gradient ∇θE(g(θ)) is correspondingly a set-valued quantity in general; any continuously tracked minimising geodesic yields a valid subgradient selection, and each element of this subdifferential is a valid descent direction for E. The squared path-length regulariser of Definition 4.4 circumvents this non- uniqueness by tracking a specific (training-induced) horizontal path on Mflow (θ0), with gradient given by Proposition F.3. RemarkC.4 (Standard ridge fails doubly in feature-learning networks).Standard ridge, Rstd(θ) = ∥θ∥2, fails in feature-learning networks for two independent reasons.First, it ignores the initialisation: for any θ0 ̸=0 , minimising ∥θ∥2 rather than ∥θ−θ 0∥2 already biases solutions away from the gradient flow limitin the kernel regime, where anchored ridge is the correct canonical regulariser [ 13, 33]. For linear models, anchored ridge is the classical Tikhonov regulariser [41] centred at the initial estimate θ0, equivalent to MAP estimation under the Gaussian parameter prior N(θ 0, 1 2λ I) [34]; ridge regression [16] corresponds to the special case θ0 =0 .Second, even for networks with zero initialisation, Rstd =R ctr|θ0=0, so Theorem 3.1 applies directly: whenever J(θ) is non-constant along the training trajectory, the minimum-Euclidean-norm interpolator centred at the origin diverges from θ⋆. In summary, anchored ridge is the correct target in the kernel regime but is itself insufficient in the feature-learning regime, where only the canonical curved lift of Definition 4.3 selects the gradient flow solution. C.5 Kernel-regime reduction of the canonical objects Proposition C.10(Kernel-regime reduction).Suppose the model has constant Jacobian J(θ)≡J on U, with K=J J ⊤ ≻0 on the corresponding NTK Gram matrix. Then the canonical objects of Section 4 reduce as follows: (a)Affine flow manifold:M flow (θ0)∩U= � θ0 + Im(J⊤)  ∩U; (b)Closed-form gauge-fixed representative:g| −1 θ0 (c) =θ 0 +J ⊤K −1(c−g(θ 0));

(c)RKHS canonical energy:E(c) = (c−g(θ 0))⊤K −1(c−g(θ 0)) =∥c−g(θ 0)∥2 RKHS; (d) Pythagorean lift:the canonical lift of Definition 4.3 satisfies R(θ) =∥θ−θ 0∥2 (anchored ridge); (e) Geodesic distance: d2 flow (θ0,θ) =∥θ−θ 0∥2 for every θ∈ Mflow (θ0), and the metric gap operatorN(c)of Theorem C.3 vanishes identically; (f) NTK Gaussian Process prior:the Riemannian Gibbs Process of Definition 4.2 reduces to g(θ)∼ N(g(θ 0),(2β) −1K), equivalentlyf(·;θ)∼ GP(f(·;θ 0),(2β) −1kNTK). Corollary C.2(Anchored ridge is the canonical lift in the kernel regime).Under the hypotheses of Proposition C.10, geodesic ridge of Proposition 4.1 coincides with anchored ridge: d2 (θ,θ 0) = ∥θ−θ 0∥2 for everyθ∈U. Corollary C.3(Riemannian Gibbs Process is the NTK Gaussian Process in the kernel regime).Under the hypotheses of Proposition C.10, the canonical function-space prior of Definition 4.2 coincides 17

with the NTK Gaussian Process posterior N(g(θ 0),(2β) −1K) on the training-output coordinates and pushes forward, under evaluation at any finite test inputs Xte ⊂ X, to a Gaussian on Y |Xte| with the standard NTK-GP mean and covariance. C.6 Failure of non-canonical regularisation in the feature-learning regime The bias of anchored ridge identified by Theorem 3.1 is not specific to the chord ∥θ−θ 0∥2: every trajectory-blindquadratic regulariser whose curvature-correcting tensor is independent of training history exhibits the same pathology, and every regulariser that depends only on outputs cannot disambiguate the gauge-fixed representative from any other point on the same fibre. Proposition C.11(Static-anchor regularisers bias gradient flow).Let M∈Sym + m and v∈R m beindependent of the gradient-flow trajectory(functions of θ0 only). Define RM,v (θ) := (θ−v) ⊤M(θ−v) and θ⋆ M,v := lim λ↓0 arg minθ[L(g(θ), Y) +λR M,v (θ)]. Then θ⋆ M,v is characterised by M(θ ⋆ M,v −v)∈Im(J � θ⋆ M,v ⊤ ) and, under Assumption 2.1, satisfies θ⋆ M,v =θ ⋆ if and only if M(θ ⋆ −v)∈Im(J(θ ⋆)⊤). In particular, whenever the gradient-flow displacement θ⋆ −θ 0 has a non-trivial component in ker(J(θ ⋆)) (the generic feature-learning regime under non-constant J(θ(t)) ), no choice of static (M,v)can satisfy this condition, so every trajectory-blind quadratic biases the gradient-flow limit. Proposition C.12(Output-norm regularisers fail to select the gauge-fixed representative).Let f: Rn →[0,∞) be continuous with f(g(θ 0)) = 0 and f strictly convex on C0(θ0). The pure output- shaped regulariserR(θ) :=f(g(θ))satisfies, for everyλ >0, arg min θ  L(g(θ), Y) +λR(θ)  = Θ (c λ)∩U, i.e. the entire output fibre at the optimal output valuecλ is selected. The vanishing-regularisation limit limλ↓0 arg min is therefore Θ⋆ ∩U , not the singleton {θ⋆}. A non-trivial fibre extension is therefore necessary in the canonical lift of Definition 4.3; pure output-energy is structurally insufficient to select the gradient-flow limit. Proposition C.13(anchored ridge lower-bounds the canonical regulariser).For every θ∈ Mflow (θ0), ∥θ−θ 0∥2 ≤d 2 flow (θ0,θ) =d 2 (θ,θ 0). The inequality is an instance of the relaxation principle: anchored ridge is the minimum ofR 1 0 ∥˙γ(t)∥2 dt over paths from θ0 to θ withoutthe horizontal constraint ˙γ∈Im(J(γ) ⊤), whereas the canonical regulariser is the minimum of the same functionalwiththe horizontal constraint imposed. Dropping a constraint can only decrease the minimum. Corollary C.4(Sandwich bound and shared vanishing-regularisation limit).For every θ∈ Mflow (θ0)reached by a piecewise-C 1 horizontal training pathγ train fromθ 0, ∥θ−θ 0∥2 ≤d 2 flow (θ0,θ) =d 2 (θ,θ 0)≤L 2(γtrain). Each of the three regularisers (anchored ridge ∥θ−θ 0∥2, geodesic ridge d2 (θ,θ 0), arc ridge L2(γtrain)) attains the same vanishing-regularisation limit when the corresponding regularised objective is minimised under exact gradient flow: limλ↓0 arg min(L(g(·), Y) +λR) =θ ⋆ for R∈ {∥· −θ0∥2 |Mflow(θ0), d2 (·,θ 0), L2(γtrain)}. The three regularisers therefore agree at the gradient-flow limit but disagree away from it; arc ridge is the minimax-optimal point of this sandwich (Proposition F .2). Proposition C.14(Properness of the Riemannian Gibbs Process).Under Assumption 2.1, the partition function of the Riemannian Gibbs Process prior of Definition 4.2 is finite: Z:=
Z C0(θ0) exp � −βE(c)  dc≤  π C2 β n/2 , where C is the upper Jacobian bound of Assumption 2.1. The Riemannian Gibbs Process is therefore a proper probability measure onC 0(θ0). 18

Proposition C.15(Kolmogorov consistency over arbitrary finite test inputs).Fix a training set X= (X 1, . . . , Xn), an initialisation θ0, and a temperature β >0. Define the parameter-space prior πβ onM flow (θ0)∩Vby πβ(dθ) =Z −1 β exp � −β d2 flow (θ0,θ)  dvolgambient(θ), where dvolgambient is the Riemannian volume measure induced by the ambient Euclidean metric. For any finite test set Xte = (Xn+1, . . . , Xn+ℓ)⊆ X, let ΦXte :M flow (θ0)∩V→R ℓ be the evaluation mapθ7→(f(X n+1;θ), . . . , f(Xn+ℓ;θ)). The push-forward family µXte := (Φ Xte )♯πβ, X te ⊆ Xfinite, is Kolmogorov consistent: for any pair Xte ⊇X ′ te, the marginal of µXte on the coordinates indexed by X ′ te equals µX ′ te. By the Kolmogorov extension theorem, the family {µXte } defines a unique stochastic process on X at the fixed training set (X,θ 0, β). (Consistencyacrosstraining subsets remains an open problem.) RemarkC.5 (Status of the Riemannian Gibbs Process as a Bayesian prior). Properness.Under Assumption 2.1 the partition function R exp(−βE(c))dc is finite (Proposition C.14); outside this regime we make no such claim.MAP well-posedness.The MAP estimator arg minθ L(g(θ), Y) + λE(g(θ)) is well-defined and finite for every λ >0 regardless of normalisation.NTK reduction.In the kernel regime, where E reduces to the squared RKHS norm, the prior is the proper Gaussian Process N(g(θ 0),(2β) −1K).Kolmogorov consistency.Verification of Kolmogorov consistency for the projective family {pX }X across input subsets remains open; however, the parametric structure buys us consistency over arbitrary finite test inputs at any fixed training set: any parameter prior on Mflow (θ0)∩V — naturally the Riemannian Gibbs measure ∝exp(−β d2 flow(θ0,·)) with respect to the induced Riemannian volume — pushed through evaluation at any finite Xte ⊂ Xyields a Kolmogorov-consistent family of finite-dimensional marginals [31, 37, 46]. D.2 Structural lemmas Lemma D.4(NHIM stable-manifold structure of Mflow (θ0)).Under Assumptions A.1 and 2.1, there exists an open neighbourhoodV⊆Uofθ ⋆ such that: (i)Θ ⋆ ∩V is an embedded C ∞ submanifold of V of codimension n, with Tθ(Θ⋆ ∩V) = kerJ(θ)at everyθ∈Θ ⋆ ∩V. (iii)Θ ⋆ ∩V is normally hyperbolic for the gradient flow vector field −∇θL, with stable bundle Im(J(·) ⊤)|Θ⋆∩V and trivial unstable bundle. The local stable manifold W s loc(θ⋆) := Mflow (θ0)∩V is an embedded C ∞ n-dimensional submanifold of V tangent at θ⋆ to Im(J(θ ⋆)⊤), varyingC ∞-smoothly with the basepoint acrossΘ ⋆ ∩V. Proof. (i)On U, J(θ) has rank n by Assumption 2.1, so Y is a regular value of g|U . The regular- value theorem [26, Cor. 5.14] gives Θ⋆ ∩U= (g| U )−1(Y) is an embedded C ∞ codimension-n submanifold withT θ(Θ⋆ ∩U) = kerJ(θ). Restrict to a relatively compactV⋐Ucontainingθ ⋆. (ii)For SSE, ∇2 θL(θ) = 2J(θ) ⊤ J(θ) + 2 P i(g(θ)−Y) i∇2gi. At θ∈Θ ⋆ residuals vanish, leaving∇ 2L= 2J(θ) ⊤ J(θ). The eigenvalue claim follows fromσ min(J(θ))≥conV. (iii)The linearisation of the gradient flow vector field −∇θL at θ∈Θ ⋆ ∩V is −2J(θ) ⊤ J(θ) : it hasnnegative eigenvalues bounded above by−2c 2 onIm(J(θ) ⊤)andm−nzero eigenvalues on Tθ(Θ⋆ ∩V) = kerJ(θ) . The zero eigenspace coincides with the tangent space of Θ⋆ ∩V , with uniform spectral gap 2c2 in the transverse direction; hence Θ⋆ ∩V is a normally hyperbolic invariant manifold for the gradient flow [42, 15, Def. 2]. The NHIM stable-manifold theorem [42, Thm. 4.1] gives a C ∞ foliation of a neighbourhood of Θ⋆ ∩V in V by stable manifolds, each an embedded C ∞ n-submanifold tangent to Im(J(·) ⊤) at its basepoint and varying smoothly with the basepoint. The leaf containing the gradient-flow trajectory from θ0 is W s loc(θ⋆) =M flow (θ0)∩V by definition of the flow manifold (Definition 3.2) and uniqueness of theω-limit. Lemma D.7(Local properness of g|Mflow(θ0)∩V ).Under Assumption 2.1, with V the neighbourhood of Lemma D.4, the restricted output mapg|Mflow(θ0)∩V :M flow (θ0)∩V→C 0(θ0)⊆R n is proper. Proof. D.4 Geometric identities Lemma D.8(Infinitesimal orthogonality).At every θ with full-rank J(θ) , Im(J(θ) ⊤)⊥kerJ(θ) in the ambient Euclidean inner product on Rm, and Rm = Im(J(θ) ⊤)⊕kerJ(θ) as an orthogonal decomposition. Proof. If v∈Im(J(θ) ⊤) and w∈kerJ(θ) , write v=J(θ) ⊤ u; then v⊤w=u ⊤J(θ)w= 0 . Dimension count: dim Im(J(θ) ⊤) = rankJ(θ) =n and dim kerJ(θ) =m−n , summing to m. Lemma D.9(Geodesic length dominates ambient chord).For any embedded C 1 submanifold M⊆R m and any two points p, q∈M in the same path component, the intrinsic geodesic distance dM (p, q)(defined as infimum of arc lengths ofC 1 curves inM) satisfiesd M (p, q)≥ ∥p−q∥. Proof. For any C 1 curve γ: [0,1]→M with γ(0) =p , γ(1) =q , Length(γ) = R 1 0 ∥˙γ(t)∥dt≥ R 1 0 ˙γ(t)dt =∥q−p∥ by the triangle inequality for integrals. Taking the infimum over γ preserves the inequality. Lemma D.10(Arc-length endpoint gradient).Let γ: [0, T]→R m be piecewise-C 1 with ˙γ(T−)̸= 0 . Then the arc length s(T) = R T 0 ∥˙γ(t)∥dt is Fréchet-differentiable with respect to the endpoint γ(T) , with ∂s ∂γ(T) = ˙γ(T−) ∥˙γ(T−)∥ . Equivalently, by the envelope theorem applied to the arc-length functional [30]: the gradient of the arc length with respect to the endpoint equals the terminal unit tangent vector. For a geodesic, this coincides with the terminal momentum (cotangent vector in the Hamiltonian formulation). Proof. Fix h∈R m and δ >0. Consider the comparison path ˜γthat agrees with γ on [0, T−δ] and connectsγ(T−δ)linearly toγ(T) +εhon[T−δ, T]. The arc length of the final segment is ˙γ(T−)δ+εh = ˙γ(T−) δ 1 + ε δ * ˙γ(T−) ∥˙γ(T−)∥2 , h + +O  ε2 δ2 !1/2 , so ∆s=s ε −s=ε⟨˙γ(T −)/∥˙γ(T −)∥, h⟩+O(ε 2/δ+εδ) . Setting δ= √ε gives ∆s= ε⟨˙γ(T−)/∥˙γ(T −)∥, h⟩+O(ε 3/2), confirming the stated Fréchet derivative. 21

D.5 Optimisation lemma Lemma D.11(Vanishing-regularisation selection principle).Let L: Θ→[0,∞) be continuous with non-empty arg minL, and let R: Θ→[0,∞] be continuous. Assume that for each λ >0 the regularised problem admits a unique minimiser ˆθλ ∈arg min(L+λR) , and that every sequence { ˆθλk } with λk ↓0 has a convergent subsequence. Then every limit point of ˆθλ as λ↓0 lies in arg minLand, amongarg minL, minimisesR. Proof. Let ¯θ∈arg minL and set L⋆ := minL . By optimality, L( ˆθλ) +λR( ˆθλ)≤ L⋆ +λR( ¯θ), so L( ˆθλ)≤ L⋆ +λR( ¯θ) and R( ˆθλ)≤(L ⋆ − L(ˆθλ))/λ+R( ¯θ)≤R( ¯θ). Taking λ↓0 along a convergent subsequence with limit θ∞, continuity yields L(θ∞)≤ L⋆, so θ∞ ∈arg minL . Passing to the limit in R( ˆθλ)≤R( ¯θ) gives R(θ∞)≤R( ¯θ) for every ¯θ∈arg minL ; hence θ∞ minimises Roverarg minL. E Proofs We restate each main-text result at the head of its proof for convenience. Proofs cite Assumptions A.1–A.4 of Appendix A and Lemmas D.1–D.11 of Appendix D by label. E.1 Proof of Proposition C.1 (gradient-flow convergence) Proof of Proposition C.1. A.1 makes the gradient-flow vector field −∇θL(g(θ), Y) locally Lips- chitz on U, so the Picard–Lindelöf theorem [21, Thm. 2.2] gives a unique maximal C 1 solution θ(·) fromθ 0. By A.4, the trajectory remains inUfor allt≥0. Output-error decay.Write r(t) :=g(θ(t))−Y . Under A.3 with the squared-error specialisation, ˙g(θ) =J(θ) ˙θ=−2K(θ)r, so d dt ∥r(t)∥2 = 2r ⊤ ˙r=−4r ⊤K(θ(t))r≤ −4c 2 ∥r(t)∥2 , where the last bound usesK(θ)⪰c 2In from σmin(J(θ))≥c on U (A.4). Grönwall’s inequality [21, Lem. A.1] then gives∥r(t)∥ 2 ≤ ∥r(0)∥2 e−4c2t, i.e.∥r(t)∥ ≤ ∥r(0)∥e−2c2t. Existence of ω-limit.A.4 gives σmax(J(θ))≤C on U, so ˙θ(t) = J(θ(t)) ⊤ 2r(t) ≤ 2C∥r(t)∥ ≤2C∥r(0)∥e −2c2t, which is Lebesgue-integrable on [0,∞) . Cauchy completeness of Rm then gives θ⋆ := limt→∞ θ(t), and continuity of g together with r(t)→0 gives g(θ ⋆) =Y , hence θ⋆ ∈Θ ⋆. By A.4, θ⋆ ∈U , and by Lemma D.4 (i), θ⋆ ∈Θ ⋆ ∩V for the neighbourhood V defined there. (For general strictly convex µ-strongly-convex M-smooth losses the argument repeats with rate2c 2µ2/Min place of2c 2.) E.2 Proof of Theorem C.4 (output-space energy uniqueness) Proof of Theorem C.4. Step 1: ρK is a function of ∥c∥2 K−1.The group GK−1 :={U∈GL(n) : U ⊤K −1U=K −1} acts transitively on each level set {c:c ⊤K −1c=s} for s≥0 : any two such vectors are related by a K −1-orthogonal transformation. Axiom (i) therefore gives ρK(c) = f(c ⊤K −1c) for some f: [0,∞)→[0,∞) with f(0) = 0; continuity of ρK implies continuity of f. Step 2: f is additive on [0,∞) .For s, t≥0 , choose u,v with u⊤K −1u=s , v⊤K −1v=t , u⊤K −1v= 0 ; such pairs exist for any s, tsince dimn≥1 and K −1 ≻0 . Then (u+v) ⊤K −1(u+ v) =s+t, so by axiom (ii),f(s+t) =f(s) +f(t). Step 3: solve Cauchy.Continuous solutions to f(s+t) =f(s) +f(t) on [0,∞) with f(0) = 0 are linear [1, Ch. 2]: f(s) =αs for some α∈R . Non-triviality and ρK ≥0 force α >0.
Hence ρK(c) =αc ⊤K −1c. 22

E.3 Proof of Proposition C.5 (naturality alone is non-unique) Proof of Proposition C.5. Fix (a, B)∈R >0 ×Sym + n . The Hessian ∇2Ra,B = 2(aIm +J ⊤BJ) is positive definite (the first term is aIm ≻0 and the second is positive semi-definite), hence Ra,B is strictly convex and coercive on Θ. By Lemmas D.11 and D.3, the vanishing-regularisation limit lies inΘ ⋆ and minimisesR a,B overΘ ⋆. Constant J gives Θ⋆ =θ lin + kerJ with θlin :=θ 0 +J ⊤K −1(Y−g(θ 0)), the kernel-regime gradient-flow interpolator (Lemma D.4, kernel-regime specialisation). Decompose any θ∈Θ ⋆ as θ=θ lin +w with w∈kerJ . Then J(θ−θ 0) =J(θ lin −θ 0) is a fixed vector independent of w, so the second term of Ra,B is constant on Θ⋆. The first term decomposes by the orthogonality kerJ⊥Im(J ⊤)of Lemma D.8 and the inclusionθ lin −θ 0 ∈Im(J ⊤): ∥θ−θ 0∥2 =∥θ lin −θ 0∥2 +∥w∥ 2 . Hence Ra,B(θ) =a∥w∥ 2 +const on Θ⋆, and the unique minimiser over Θ⋆ is w= 0 , i.e. θ=θ lin. Since the kernel-regime gradient-flow trajectory is the constant-velocity straight line from θ0 to θlin (Lemma D.4, kernel specialisation), θ⋆ =θ lin, and the vanishing-regularisation limit equals θ⋆ for every(a, B). The map (a, B)7→ ∇2Ra,B = 2(aIm +J ⊤BJ) is injective on R>0 ×Sym + n : the trace of aIm + J ⊤BJ pins a given Tr(J⊤BJ) = Tr(KB) (knowable up to a), and K≻0 makes the map B7→J ⊤BJ injective on Sym+ n . Hence distinct (a, B)give distinct Hessians, distinct quadratic forms Ra,B, and distinct Gaussian MAP priors N(θ 0, 1 2λ (aIm +J ⊤BJ) −1). The family is therefore uncountable, all members satisfy naturality, and only the special case B= 0 recovers anchored ridge (up to the scale a). The orthogonal-additivity axiom of Theorem C.4 (acting on the output channel g(θ) rather than on θ directly) is what eliminates every non-trivial B to single out anchored ridge. E.4 Proof of Proposition C.10 (kernel-regime reduction) Proof of Proposition C.10.Throughout,J(θ)≡Jis constant onUandK=J J ⊤ ≻0. (a) Affine flow manifold.Constant J makes the parameter dynamics ˙θ=J ⊤u confined to θ0 + Im(J ⊤) for any L2 control u; hence Oflow (θ0)⊆θ 0 + Im(J⊤). The reverse inclusion follows from constant-control reachability: every point θ0 +J ⊤a is hit by u(t)≡a/T at time T . The basin sheet Mflow (θ0) then equals the connected component of θ0 + Im(J⊤) containing the trajectory, which (by linearity of the GF dynamics on this affine subspace) is the whole subspace intersected withU. (b) Closed-form gauge-fixed representative.On the affine flow manifold, g(θ) =g(θ 0) +J(θ−θ 0). Setting g(θ) =c and requiring θ−θ 0 ∈Im(J ⊤) gives θ−θ 0 =J ⊤a with J J⊤a=Ka= c−g(θ 0), soa=K −1(c−g(θ 0))andg| −1 θ0 (c) =θ 0 +J ⊤K −1(c−g(θ 0)). (c) RKHS canonical energy.The differential Dg|−1 θ0 (c) =J ⊤K −1 is constant. The pullback metric on C0(θ0) is therefore G= (J ⊤K −1)⊤(J ⊤K −1) =K −1J J⊤K −1 =K −1, constant in c. Geodesics of a constant Riemannian metric are constant-speed straight

lines (Definition: a curve with vanishing covariant acceleration on (Rn, K−1) is exactly an affine line [9, Ch. 2, Prop. 3.5]). The constant-speed straight line from g(θ 0) to c has G-energy R 1 0 (c−g(θ 0))⊤K −1(c−g(θ 0))dt= (c−g(θ 0))⊤K −1(c−g(θ 0)) =∥c−g(θ 0)∥2 RKHS. (d) Pythagorean lift.By Lemma D.8, θ−θ 0 decomposes orthogonally as (g|−1 θ0 (g(θ))−θ 0) + (θ−g| −1 θ0 (g(θ))) with components in Im(J ⊤) and kerJ respectively; the Pythagorean identity gives ∥θ−θ 0∥2 = g|−1 θ0 (g(θ))−θ 0 2 + θ−g| −1 θ0 (g(θ)) 2 . Using (b), g|−1 θ0 (g(θ))−θ 0 = J ⊤K −1(g(θ)−g(θ 0)), whose squared Euclidean norm equals (g(θ)−g(θ 0))⊤K −1(g(θ)− g(θ 0)) =E(g(θ)) from (c). The path-infimum on the fibre Θ (g(θ))∩V from g|−1 θ0 (g(θ)) to θ is realised by the ambient-Euclidean straight line, of squared length θ−g| −1 θ0 (g(θ)) 2 . Hence the canonical liftR(θ) =E(g(θ)) + θ−g| −1 θ0 (g(θ)) 2 =∥θ−θ 0∥2, anchored ridge. 23

(e) Geodesic distance and vanishing metric gap.For any θ∈ Mflow (θ0), the geodesic from θ0 to θ on the affine (Mflow (θ0), gambient) is the ambient straight line, of squared length ∥θ−θ 0∥2. By Theorem C.3, the metric gap operator N(c) = (I−P g|−1 θ0 (c))Dg| −1 θ0 (c) vanishes precisely when ImDg| −1 θ0 (c)⊆Im(J ⊤). Constant J gives Dg|−1 θ0 (c) =J ⊤K −1, whose image is Im(J ⊤), so N≡0on all ofC 0(θ0). (f) NTK Gaussian Process prior.The Riemannian Gibbs measure of Definition 4.2 on C0(θ0) has density p(c)∝exp(−β∥c−g(θ 0)∥2 K−1 ) by (c), which is the Gaussian den- sity N(g(θ 0),(2β) −1K). The corresponding parametric pushforward through f(·;θ) at any test inputs Xte = (X n+1, . . . , Xn+ℓ) is, in the constant-Jacobian regime, the Gaussian N � f(X te;θ 0),(2β) −1 JteJ ⊤(J J⊤)−1J J⊤ te  with Jte the Jacobian at the test inputs. The covari- ance kernel k(x,x ′) =∇ θf(x;θ 0)⊤ ∇θf(x ′;θ 0) pre- and post-conditioned through the training set agrees with the conventional NTK posterior (37, Eq. 2.41); collecting all test inputs yields the full Gaussian processGP(f(·;θ 0),(2β) −1kNTK). E.5 Proof of Corollary C.2 (anchored ridge is the canonical lift in the kernel regime) Proof of Corollary C.2. By Proposition C.10(d), the canonical lift R(θ) =∥θ−θ 0∥2 on Mflow (θ0)∪ {Θ (g(θ))}θ. Geodesic ridge of Proposition 4.1 is by definition the same lift R when the flow component is computed as squared geodesic distance on Mflow (θ0) and the fibre component along Θ (g(θ)) . Proposition C.10(e) identifies the squared geodesic distance with the squared ambient chord; Lemma D.8 identifies the fibre component with the squared ambient distance tog| −1 θ0 (g(θ)). The two components reassemble into∥θ−θ 0∥2 as in (d). E.6 Proof of Corollary C.3 (Riemannian Gibbs Process is the NTK GP) Proof of Corollary C.3. By Proposition C.10(f), the Riemannian Gibbs Process density on training- output coordinates is the Gaussian N(g(θ 0),(2β) −1K). For any finite test inputs Xte ⊆ X, evaluation of f(·;θ) at Xte is, under constant Jacobian, an affine map θ7→f(X te;θ 0) +Jte(θ−θ 0) with Jte ∈R |Xte|×m the joint output Jacobian. Affine pushforwards of Gaussians are Gaussian (standard result), giving the joint NTK GP marginal at the union X∪X te. The conditional on g(θ) =Ythen matches the standard GP posterior under the NTK kernel [37]. E.7 Proof of Theorem C.2 (local manifold structure) Proof of Theorem C.2. Conclusions (i), (ii), (iii) follow directly from Lemma D.4 (parts (i), (ii), (iii)). For the final claim, g|Mflow(θ0)∩V is a local C ∞ diffeomorphism: at any θ∈ Mflow (θ0)∩V , the dif- ferential dgθ|Tθ (Mflow(θ0)∩V) equals J(θ) restricted to Im(J(θ) ⊤) (using Tθ⋆ (Mflow (θ0)∩V) = Im(J(θ ⋆)⊤) from Lemma D.4(iii) and continuity for nearby θ). J(θ)| Im(J(θ) ⊤) is a linear isomor- phism onto Rn since σmin(J(θ))≥c on V (Assumption 2.1) and Lemma D.8 gives the orthogonal decomposition Rm = Im(J(θ) ⊤)⊕kerJ(θ) . By the inverse function theorem, g|Mflow(θ0)∩V is a local C ∞ diffeomorphism. Properness of g|Mflow(θ0)∩V on a possibly smaller relatively com- pact

neighbourhood follows from Lemma D.7; combined with the local-diffeomorphism property, g|Mflow(θ0)∩V is then a C ∞ diffeomorphism onto its open imageC0(θ0) :=g(M flow (θ0)∩V) . ranges in kerJ � g|−1 θ0 (c)  . Hence the trajectory’s G-energy reduces to ˙c⊤A⊤ ∥ A∥ ˙c= ˙c⊤K(θ(t)) −1 ˙c. E.9 Proof of Proposition C.8 (output fibres) Proof of Proposition C.8.Immediate from Lemma D.5. E.10 Proof of Corollary C.1 (fibre–flow intersection inV) Proof of Corollary C.1. By Theorem C.2, g|Mflow(θ0)∩V is a C ∞ diffeomorphism onto C0(θ0), hence a bijection. For each c∈C 0(θ0) the preimage (g|Mflow(θ0)∩V )−1(c) is a singleton, denoted g|−1 θ0 (c). Setting c=Y identifies θ′(Y) as the unique interpolator on Mflow (θ0)∩V . Convergence of the gradient flow trajectory to a point in Θ⋆ ∩V is part of Assumption 2.1; uniqueness of the limit onM flow (θ0)∩Videntifiesω(θ 0) =θ ⋆ :=θ ′(Y). E.11 Proof of Theorem 4.2 (canonical regulariser characterisation) Proof of Theorem 4.2.(a)Fixc∈C 0(θ0)andθ∈Θ (c)∩V. By Definition 4.3, d2 (θ,θ 0) =d 2 flow � θ0, g|−1 θ0 (c)  +d 2 fibre � g|−1 θ0 (c),θ  , where the first term depends only on c, and the second is θ−g| −1 θ0 (c) 2 ≥0 with equality iff θ=g| −1 θ0 (c). The unique minimiser overΘ (c)∩Vis thereforeg| −1 θ0 (c). (b)The gradient flow trajectory enters V in finite time (Assumption 2.1); thereafter θ(t)∈ Mflow (θ0)∩V and Corollary C.1 gives θ(t) =θ ′(g(θ(t))) . Hence d2 fibre (θ′(g(θ(t))),θ(t)) = 0 andd 2 (θ(t),θ 0) =d 2 flow (θ0,θ(t)) =E(g(θ(t)))by Definition 4.1. (c)Apply Lemma D.11: d2 (·,θ 0) is continuous on V (smoothness of dflow (·,·) on the smooth Riemannian manifold Mflow (θ0)∩V and dfibre (θ′(·),·) as ambient Euclidean), and tends to +∞ outside V by definition. For λ >0 small, d2 (θλ,θ 0)≤d 2 (θ⋆,θ 0)<∞ forces θλ ∈V ;

local properness (Lemma D.7) plus boundedness of the ambient Euclidean component along the flow normals confines θλ to a compact set. Lemma D.11 then gives θλ →θ ⋆ as λ↓0 , since (a) identifies θ⋆ =θ ′(Y)as the unique minimiser ofd 2 (·,θ 0)overΘ ⋆ ∩V. E.12 Proof of Theorem C.6 (uniqueness of the canonical lift) Proof of Theorem C.6. Axiom (a) writes R(θ) =E(g(θ)) +S(θ) with S a function of the on- fibre displacement θ−g| −1 θ0 (g(θ)) alone. Restricting attention to a fibre Θ (g(θ))∩V , we may 25

parametrise by piecewise-C 1 paths ψ: [0,1]→Θ (g(θ)) with ψ(0) =g| −1 θ0 (g(θ)) , ψ(1) =θ . Axiom (b) says S is a quadratic functional on each fibre; in the ambient Euclidean metric ∥·∥2 inherited from Rm, the unique quadratic functional vanishing at one endpoint and depending only on the path-equivalence class of ψ is the squared length of the minimising fibre geodesic, namely inf ψ R 1 0 ˙ψ(t) 2 dt, by Proposition C.9 applied to Θ (g(θ))∩V with the ambient Euclidean metric. Axiom (c) fixes the additive constant:S(g| −1 θ0 (g(θ))) = 0. Existence is clear (the formula in the conclusion satisfies (a)–(c) and is finite by Lemma D.7 and Remark C.2). For uniqueness, suppose R′ also satisfies (a)–(c). Decompose R′(θ) =E(g(θ)) + S′(θ) by (a). Restricting to a fibre, S′ is a quadratic functional in the ambient Euclidean metric vanishing at g|−1 θ0 (g(θ)) . The classical reduction of quadratic forms in Euclidean space [26, Ch. 10], combined with the absence of cross-terms (a single-endpoint quadratic on a fibre with fixed reference g|−1 θ0 (g(θ)) ), gives S′(θ) =α d2 fibre � g|−1 θ0 (g(θ)),θ  for some α≥0 . Non-negativity of R′ forces α >0; absorbing α into the global temperature β of Definition 4.2 (which is the only free scale parameter in the canonical objects, by gradient-flow scale invariance) recoversα= 1 . Hence R=R ′. In the kernel regime, Proposition C.10(d) collapses this to anchored ridge; in the feature-learning regime, the flow component d2 flow � g|−1 θ0 (g(θ)),θ 0  replaces the Euclidean chord, giving geodesic ridge of Proposition 4.1. E.13 Proof of Theorem C.7 (combined uniqueness of geodesic ridge) Proof of Theorem C.7. Apply Theorem C.4 with G=K −1 at θ0: the function-space-energy axioms identify E(g(θ)) uniquely up to scale. Apply Theorem C.6: given this E, the lift axioms (a)–(c) identify R(θ) =E(g(θ)) +d 2 fibre � g|−1 θ0 (g(θ)),θ  uniquely up to a positive scale. Theorem 4.2(c) shows this R has the vanishing-regularisation property; by Lemma D.11 the property identifies the gradient-flow limit uniquely onΘ ⋆ ∩V. By definition d2 flow � g|−1 θ0 (g(θ)),θ 0  ≡E(g(θ)) (Proposition 4.1 together with Definition 4.1; the canonical energy is the squared geodesic distance on Mflow (θ0) from θ0 to g|−1 θ0 (g(θ)) by construction of the energy as a constrained kinetic-energy minimisation). Substitution gives the stated formR(θ) =d 2 flow � θ0, g|−1 θ0 (g(θ))  +d 2 fibre � g|−1 θ0 (g(θ)),θ  =d 2 (θ,θ 0). In the kernel regime, Proposition C.10(d) collapses R to anchored ridge and Corollary C.3 collapses the Riemannian Gibbs Process to the NTK Gaussian Process. E.14 Proof of Proposition C.9 (constant-speed optimality) Proof of Proposition C.9. Let γ: [0,1]→R m be a C 1 path with fixed endpoints and length L:= R 1 0 ∥˙γ(t)∥dt. By Cauchy–Schwarz, L2 = Z 1 0 ∥˙γ(t)∥ ·1dt 2 ≤ Z 1 0 ∥˙γ(t)∥2 dt· Z 1 0 12 dt=E(γ), with equality iff ∥˙γ(t)∥is constant in t. Hence among all parametrisations of any fixed image-curve (with time budget T= 1

), energy E is minimised by constant-speed parametrisations, and at that minimiser E=L 2. Minimising over image-curves as well selects the length-minimising (geodesic) image, provingE(γ opt) =L 2(γopt). E.15 Proof of Proposition C.13 (anchored ridge lower bound via relaxation) Proof of Proposition C.13. Let θ∈ M flow (θ0). By Definition 4.3 and dfibre (θ,θ) = 0 , d2 (θ,θ 0) =d 2 flow (θ0,θ) . Consider two minimisation problems over paths γ: [0,1]→R m withγ(0) =θ 0,γ(1) =θ: (Puncon) : min γ Z 1 0 ∥˙γ(t)∥2 dt, (Phor) : min γ Z 1 0 ∥˙γ(t)∥2 dts.t.γ(t)∈ M flow (θ0)and˙γ(t)∈Im(J(γ(t)) ⊤). 26

Problem (Puncon) is solved by the constant-speed straight line with optimal value∥θ−θ 0∥2. Problem (Phor) is the energy-minimisation over horizontal paths on Mflow (θ0), solved by the constant-speed geodesic on Mflow (θ0) with optimal value d2 flow (θ0,θ) (Proposition C.9). Since (Phor) has a strictly smaller feasible set than(P uncon), val(Phor)≥val(P uncon), i.e.d 2 flow (θ0,θ)≥ ∥θ−θ 0∥2. E.16 Proof of Corollary C.4 (sandwich bound and shared vanishing-reg limit) Proof of Corollary C.4. The lower bound ∥θ−θ 0∥2 ≤d 2 flow (θ0,θ) is Proposition C.13. E.22 Proof of Proposition C.15 (Kolmogorov consistency over finite test inputs) Proof of Proposition C.15. By Theorem C.2, Mflow (θ0)∩V is a smooth n-dimensional Riemannian manifold and the Riemannian volume measure dvolgambient is well-defined on it. By Proposition C.14 (and the change of variables θ=g| −1 θ0 (c) pushing dvol to p detG(c)dc on C0(θ0)), the normal- ising constant Zβ := R Mflow(θ0)∩V exp(−β d2 flow (θ0,θ))dvol gambient(θ) is finite, so πβ is a Borel probability measure onM flow (θ0)∩V. For each finite Xte ⊆ X, the evaluation map ΦXte :M flow (θ0)∩V→R |Xte| is continuous (A.1) and Borel-measurable, so the push-forward µXte := (ΦXte )♯πβ is a Borel probability measure on R|Xte|. Consistency.Suppose X ′ te ⊆X te and let π:R |Xte| →R |X ′ te| be the coordinate projection onto the indices inX ′ te. By construction,Φ X ′ te =π◦Φ Xte. Functoriality of push-forward gives π♯µXte =π ♯(ΦXte )♯πβ = (π◦Φ Xte )♯πβ = (Φ X ′ te )♯πβ =µ X ′ te . Hence the family {µXte }Xte⊆Xfinite satisfies the Kolmogorov consistency condition. By the Kol- mogorov extension theorem [31, Thm. A.6], there exists a unique stochastic process (Fx)x∈X on X at the fixed training set whose finite-dimensional marginals are exactly {µXte }. Consistencyacross training subsets requires verifying compatibility of the parameter-space measure πβ as the training setXvaries, which is genuinely additional structure not provided by this construction. F Detailed Analysis of Arc Ridge Regularisation We provide detailed derivations justifying the squared path-length regulariser R=L 2(γtrain) = s(T) 2, where s(T) = R T 0 ˙θ(t) dt is the cumulative Euclidean arc length of the training path. Throughout, we work in effective-parameter-space (parameters scaled by µP widths as in Section 4) and suppress the subscriptefffor readability. F.1 Detailed Derivation of the Upper Bound We expand the proof of Theorem 4.3 and clarify when the bound is tight. Proposition F.1(Upper bound via constant-speed optimality, expanded).Let γ: [0, T]→ Mflow (θ0) be any piecewise-C1 horizontal path fromθ 0 toθ∈ Mflow (θ0). Then d2 flow (θ0,θ)≤L 2(γ) = Z T 0 ∥˙γ(t)∥dt !2 . Equality holds if and only if γ is a geodesic on Mflow (θ0) (traversed at any constant or non-constant speed). Proof. Step 1: Geodesic distance as infimum of arc lengths.The geodesic distance dflow (θ0,θ) equals the infimum of arc lengths L(γ) = R T 0 ∥˙γ(t)∥dt over all piecewise-C 1 horizontal paths γ from θ0 to θ; this is the standard definition of Riemannian distance with the ambient Euclidean metric restricted toM flow (θ0). Therefore, for any admissible pathγ, dflow (θ0,θ)≤L(γ)≤L(γ train), where the second inequality holds because γtrain is itself an admissible horizontal path. Squaring both sides givesd 2 flow (θ0,θ)≤L 2(γtrain). Step 2: Tightness condition.Equality d2 flow (θ0,θ) =L 2(γ) holds if and only if L(γ) =d flow (θ0,θ) , i.e. γ is a length-minimising path. By Proposition C.9, among all paths of fixed total arc length, the minimum energy E(γ) = R ∥˙γ∥2 dt is achieved by the constant-speed reparametrisation; length- minimising paths are exactly the geodesics, regardless of their time-parametrisation. Since L2(γ) =
(arc length)2 depends only on the set-theoretic image ofγ, equality holds for any time-parametrisation of a geodesic. 29

Step 3: Inclusion of the fibre term.When θ(T) lies exactly on Mflow (θ0) (as guaranteed by Corollary C.1 under exact gradient flow), the canonical regulariser satisfies d2 (θ(T),θ 0) = d2 flow (θ0,θ(T)) , and the bound applies directly. In the discrete-step setting, θ(T) may deviate slightly off the manifold, contributing a non-negative fibre term d2 fibre (θ′(g(θ(T))),θ(T)) ; the path length still upper-bounds the total canonical regulariser since it upper-bounds the flow component alone. F.2 Minimax Robust Motivation Propositions C.13 and F.1 establish the sandwich ∥θ−θ 0∥2 ≤d 2 (θ,θ 0)≤L 2(γtrain) =:s(T) 2, which holds for all θ∈ Mflow (θ0) at any time T . The canonical regulariser d2 (θ,θ 0) is intractable, but at each training steptthe accumulated path lengths(t)is known exactly. Proposition F.2(Minimax robust regulariser).Fix λ >0 and let the unknown regulariser value r⋆ =d 2 (θ,θ 0) be known only to lie in the interval [rlo, rhi] = [∥θ−θ 0∥2 , s(T)2] (from the sandwich). The solution to the minimax problem min θ max r∈[rlo, rhi]  L(g(θ), Y) +λr  uses the worst-case regulariserr=r hi =s(T) 2 and is therefore equivalent to minimising the path- length regularised objective L(g(θ), Y) +λs(T) 2. Squared path length is thus the minimax-optimal practical regulariser within the sandwich. Proof. For any fixedθ, the map r7→ L(g(θ), Y)+λr is linear and strictly increasing inr (since λ > 0). Therefore, maxr∈[rlo,rhi][L(g(θ), Y) +λr] =L(g(θ), Y) +λr hi =L(g(θ), Y) +λs(T) 2, attained atr=s(T) 2. Minimising overθgives the stated equivalence. The choice λ=σ 2/n—where σ2 is the observation noise variance and n is the number of training points—has a Bayesian interpretation as the ratio of the likelihood precision to the prior precision in MAP inference. Under a Gaussian likelihood with noise σ (i.e., the SSE setting of A.3 paired with L(g(θ), Y) as −logp(Y|g(θ)) up to constants), this λ makes the regularisation gradient dimensionally consistent with the data gradient (both scale as 1/n in the large-data limit), and the stopping criterion derived in Section F.4 below requires only an estimate ofσ2 rather than a validation set. For other strictly convex losses, the analogous coupling reads λ∝β/(likelihood precision) , with the prior scaleβof Definition 4.1 substituted for the Gaussian-specific factor1/(2σ 2). F.3 Gradient Under Standard Gradient Flow We derive the regularisation gradient∇ θR=∇ θs(T) 2 in the case of unmodified gradient flow. Proposition F.3(Path-length regularisation gradient).Under gradient flow ˙θ(t) =−∇ θL(g(θ), Y) , the gradient of the squared path-length regulariser R(T) =s(T) 2 with respect to the endpoint θ(T) is ∇θ(T) R= 2s(T)· ˙θ(T) ˙θ(T) =− 2s(T) ∥∇θL∥ · ∇θL θ(T) . This gradient lies in Im(J(θ(T)) ⊤), has magnitude 2s(T) , and is anti-parallel to the data gradient. Proof. Write s(T) = R T 0 ˙θ(t) dt. By Lemma D.10, ∂s/∂θ(T) = ˙θ(T)/ ˙θ(T) , the unit tangent at the endpoint. Applying the chain rule: ∇θ(T) R= 2s(T)·∂s/∂θ(T) = 2s(T) ˙θ(T)/ ˙θ(T) . Under gradient flow ˙θ=−∇ θL, so ˙θ/ ˙θ =−∇ θL/∥∇ θL∥. Since gradient flow satisfies ˙θ=−∇ θL=J(θ) ⊤ u for some u (Lemma D.1), we have ˙θ∈Im(J(θ) ⊤); hence the
gradient lies inIm(J(θ) ⊤) =T θ(T) Mflow (θ0)and has no fibre component, as claimed by Theorem 4.3. 30

Two properties of this gradient deserve emphasis: • Monotonically increasing magnitude: s(T) grows throughout training, so ∥∇R∥= 2s(T) increases over time regardless of whether the loss is decreasing. • No fibre bias: ∇R∈Im(J(θ) ⊤), so regularisation byλR introduces no gradient component inker(J(θ)); it does not pushθoff the flow manifold. F.4 Formal Equivalence to Early Stopping Under Gradient Flow Proposition F.4(Path-length regularisation is equivalent to early stopping).Assume additionally that L(·, Y)is strictly convex in model outputs (i.e. c7→ L(c, Y)is strictly convex on Y n; this holds for all standard losses including SSE, softmax cross-entropy, and logistic loss). Under gradient flow with regularised objective L(g(θ), Y) +λR , where R=s(T) 2, the training trajectory {θ(t)}t≥0 is identical (as a set-valued path in parameter-space) to the unregularised gradient flow trajectory. (In particular, s(T) is the arc length of the path-image, which depends only on the path geometry and not on its time parametrisation; it is therefore the same quantity whether computed along the regularised or unregularised trajectory.) The dynamics converge at the unique stopping timeT ⋆ satisfying 2λs(T ⋆) = ∇θL � θ(T ⋆)  ,(1) at which the total gradient is zero. Moreover,T ⋆ <∞wheneverλ >0. Proof.By Proposition F.3, the total regularised gradient is ∇θ  L+λR  =∇ θL −2λs(T) ∥∇θL∥ · ∇θL=  1− 2λs(T) ∥∇θL∥  ∇θL. As long as 2λs(t)<∥∇ θL(θ(t))∥, the factor in parentheses is positive, so ∇θ[L+λR] is a positive scalar multiple of ∇θL. Gradient flow in the direction −∇θ[L+λR] is therefore identical in direction (though not in speed) to unregularised gradient flow in direction −∇θL; the trajectory is the same path. To show T ⋆ exists and is finite: s(t) is non-decreasing and tends to ∞ along a trajectory that does not converge (since ˙θ =∥∇ θL∥ is bounded away from zero until convergence). By A.3, ∇θL(θ) = 0 if and only if g(θ) =Y (Lemma D.3); under Assumption 2.1, the Grönwall bound in Lemma D.6 gives ∥∇θL(θ(t))∥= J(θ(t)) ⊤ ∇h(g(θ(t))) ≤σ maxM∥r(t)∥ →0exponentially (with h:=L(·, Y) ). Therefore the continuous function h(t) = 2λs(t)− ∥∇θL(θ(t))∥ satisfies h(0)<0 and h(t)→+∞ ; by the intermediate value theorem there exists T ⋆ with h(T ⋆) = 0, i.e. condition (1) holds. Comparison with classical early stopping.Proposition F.4 shows that path-length regularisation is acontinuous-time, gradient-basedanalogue of early stopping: (i) Same trajectory: the model visits the same sequence of parameter values as unregularised training, only stopping earlier. (ii) No validation set: the stopping condition (1) depends only on training quantities—the cumulative arc length s(T) , the current data-loss gradient magnitude, and λ. Classical early stopping requires a held-out validation set. (iii) Interpretable via noise: setting λ=σ 2/n links the stopping criterion to the observation noise σ2: training stops at T ⋆ with s(T ⋆) =∥∇ θL(θ(T ⋆))∥ ·n/(2σ2). An estimate of σ2 from the likelihood model or held-out noise is sufficient. (iv) Grokking avoidance: the path length s(t) is monotone; as training approaches the interpolat- ing manifold, ∥∇θL∥ →0while s continues to grow, ensuring that T ⋆ lies strictly before full interpolation for anyλ >0. G Experimental Details We discuss

here the main details surrounding the two large-scale experiments in Section 5. We share our codebase at https://github.com/GWhittle110/canonical-regularization/, which is predominantly based on the PyTorch [35] and mup [45] libraries. 31

G.1 UTKFace For this experiment, we adapt the ResNet18 architecture [ 14] by replacing the classification head with a MuReadout regression head using default Pytorch Linear initialisation (kaiming uniform). We transfer learn from pretraining with no weight freezing, ensuring µP via per-layer learning rate scaling. We train each regulariser setup over 3 seeds for 50 epochs with a batch size of 128, base learning rate of 0.003 (decreased to 0.0003 for the largest regularisation strength due to instability; early stopping prevents non-regulariser-induced undertraining here nonetheless), and momentum of 0.9 (to account for stochasticity induced by minibatching). We report test performance from the designated evaluation dataset [53], and use an 80-20 validation split for early stopping. We further corrupt targets with a per-datapoint fixed (for a given training run) additive isotropic 0-mean Gaussian noise with a standard deviation of 0.3 years. G.2 Yelp Review For this experiment, we use the DistilBERT [39] encoder base architecture, again with a MuReadout regression head using default Pytorch Linear initialisation (kaiming uniform). We transfer learn from pretraining again with with no weight freezing, ensuring µP via per-layer learning rate scaling. We train each regulariser setup over 2 seeds (due to excess runtime) over a number of epochs determined using the patience mechanism, that is, we train for a base 20 epochs, then subsequently whenever the final epoch exhibits the best validation loss we train for a further three epochs, again due to the excessive runtime preventing substantial overtraining. We again train with a base learning rate of 0.003 (this time decreasing to 0.0001 for the largest regularisation strength) and momentum of 0.9. We further process the training dataset by first transforming it to a valid regression target by normalising the scores (1 star to 5 star) to the interval [0, 1] by affine transformation, transforming to the reals via logit transform (clipping scores at 0.01 and 0.99 to prevent infinite targets), then corruption by with a per-datapoint fixed (for a given training run) additive isotropic 0-mean Gaussian noise with a standard deviation of 0.1 (unitless). G.3 Compute Both experiments were carried out remotely on Modal A10 GPUs. The UTKFace experiments run relatively quickly at roughly 10 minutes per regulariser per seed, while the Yelp Review experiment is considerably slower at roughly 5 hours per regulariser per seed. Total project compute spend was roughly $315 up to the point of submission. H Empirical Verification of Assumption 2.1 We probe Assumption 2.1 (uniform Jacobian conditioning) directly along gradient-flow trajectories. Recall the assumption claims the existence of constants 0< c≤C <∞ such that c≤σ min(J(θ)) and σmax(J(θ))≤C for all θ∈U , with U an open neighbourhood of Θ⋆ traversed by the trajectory. Equivalently, the NTK Gram K(θ) =J(θ)J(θ) ⊤ has σ2 min(J(θ))≤λ min(K(θ)) and λmax(K(θ))≤σ 2 max(J(θ)) , so monitoring λmin(K(θ)) and λmax(K(θ)) along training is a direct empirical proxy. Setup.We train a µP -parameterised MLP with two hidden layers and GELU activations [ 45, 48] on the 1-D regression toy dataset D={(x i, yi)}16 i=1 with xi ∈[−1,1] uniformly spaced, yi = sin( π 2 xi) +ε i for

εi ∼ N(0, σ2) with σ small. Training uses full-batch gradient descent (a discrete-time approximation of gradient flow) with no regularisation (Ridgeless), at hidden widths dmodel ∈ {64,256,1024,4096,16384} across multiple seeds. At each epoch we recordλmin(K(θ)) , λmax(K(θ)) , and the training loss; epochwise quantities are aggregated across seeds with shaded confidence regions denoting standard error. Results.Figure 4 shows the three quantities. Two qualitative observations support Assumption 2.1: (i) the minimum NTK eigenvalue λmin(K(θ)) remains bounded away from 0 throughout training, with stable properties across width and growth to a stable maximum across training; (ii) the maximum NTK eigenvalue λmax(K(θ)) remains finite throughout training, with width- dependent magnitude that stabilises asd model → ∞; 32

(iii) the training loss decreases monotonically, consistent with exponential convergence of gradient flow to an interpolator under Assumption 2.1 (Proposition C.1). The combination of (i) a strict positive lower bound on λmin(K(θ)) along the trajectory and (ii) a uniform upper bound on λmax(K(θ)) is exactly the spectral condition required by Assumption 2.1; (iii) verifies the trajectory remains in U until convergence. Together these provide preliminary empiri- cal evidence that Assumption 2.1 holds across widths in the practical µP regime, complementing the theoretical justification in Appendix A (A.4) and Remark A.1. A more comprehensive verification across architectures and tasks remains a natural avenue for follow-up work. 0 100000 200000 300000 400000 500000 Epoch 0 10 5 10 4 10 3 10 2 10 1 min(K( t)) d = 64 d = 256 d = 1024 d = 4096 d = 16384 0 100000 200000 300000 400000 500000 Epoch 102 103 max(K( t)) d = 64 d = 256 d = 1024 d = 4096 d = 16384 0 100000 200000 300000 400000 500000 Epoch 10 4 10 3 10 2 10 1 (g( ), Y) d = 64 d = 256 d = 1024 d = 4096 d = 16384 Figure 4: Empirical verification of Assumption 2.1 along gradient-flow trajectories of a 2-hidden- layer µP MLP with GELU activations on a 16-point 1-D regression toy (y= sin( π 2 x) +ε), at widths dmodel ∈ {64,256,1024,4096,16384}.Left: minimum NTK eigenvalue λmin(K(θ)) over training (corresponds to the lower spectral bound 2c2 in Assumption 2.1).Centre: maximum NTK eigenvalue λmax(K(θ)) over training (corresponds to the upper spectral bound 2C 2).Right: training loss over training. Both spectral quantities remain bounded throughout training and across widths; the loss decreases monotonically, consistent with the exponential-convergence prediction of Proposition C.1. 33

Wasserstein bounds for denoising diffusion probabilistic models via the F¨ollmer process Yuta Koike *† May 19, 2026 Abstract This paper studies sampling error bounds for denoising diffusion probabilistic models (DDPMs) in the 2-Wasserstein distance. Our contributions are threefold. (i) Under general Lipschitz-type conditions on the score function and for a broad class of variance schedules, including the cosine schedule, we es- tablish sharp upper bounds that are optimal in both the dimension and the number of steps, and recover several sharp error bounds previously obtained in the literature. (ii) We prove that the same Lipschitz- type conditions, which encompass those commonly imposed on the (learned) score, imply a logarithmic Sobolev inequality and hence a quadratic transportation cost inequality for the DDPM. As a consequence, in settings covered by existing work, an optimal Wasserstein bound, up to a logarithmic factor, follows from the recently obtained sharp error bound in the Kullback–Leibler divergence under geometric-type variance schedules. (iii) We show that for general log-concave target distributions, the optimal Wasser- stein error bound remains attainable even without a quadratic transportation cost inequality for the target. Our analysis is based on viewing the DDPM sampler as a discretization of the F ¨ollmer process rather than the conventional reverse Ornstein–Uhlenbeck process. Keywords: cosine schedule, initialization, log-concave distribution, quadratic transportation cost inequal- ity, sampling error, stochastic localization. 1 Introduction Over the last decade, we have witnessed dramatic progress in generative artificial intelligence (AI) and its successful applications across a wide range of fields. This paper focuses on generative models, the mathematical foundation of generative AI. Mathematically speaking, generative models aim to construct a sampling procedure for an unknown probability distributionP ∗ given training data drawn fromP ∗.Score- based generative models(SGMs), also known asdiffusion models, construct such a sampler by estimating the score functionofP ∗. Owing to their high sample quality and diversity, SGMs are now widely regarded as a leading class of generative models for various tasks, particularly in image generation [8, 15, 67]. At the same time, sampling from a trained SGM is computationally more expensive than competing approaches such as generative adversarial networks (GANs) and variational autoencoders (V AEs), since it requires iterative *Graduate School of Mathematical Sciences, University of Tokyo †CREST, Japan Science and Technology Agency 1 arXiv:2605.18069v1 [stat.ML] 18 May 2026

evaluations of the learned score function [66]. This computational bottleneck naturally motivates the study of sampling errors for SGMs. In this paper, we develop sampling error bounds for thedenoising diffusion probabilistic model(DDPM) proposed in [26, 58], which is one of the most widely used SGMs. Previous works have mainly analyzed sampling errors for the DDPM in terms of total variation (TV) distance and Kullback–Leibler (KL) diver- gence [5, 10, 13, 29, 30, 31, 37, 40, 41, 42, 43], yielding sharp bounds under fairly general assumptions on P ∗. By contrast, bounds in the 2-Wasserstein distance have recently attracted increasing attention due to their statistical relevance (see [2, Section 3.4] and [6, Section 1.2]). As discussed in [2, Section 3.5], analyzing sampling errors in Wasserstein distance is inherently more challenging than in TV distance or KL divergence. Although there is a rapidly growing body of work in this direction [2, 6, 7, 21, 22, 50, 61, 62, 65, 68], the problem remains far from being fully understood. We refer to Table 1 in [61] for a comprehensive summary of existing results. The aim of this paper is to advance this line of research by providing new theoretical insights into sampling error bounds in Wasserstein distance for the DDPM. Specifically, our contributions are threefold. First, we establish sharp upper bounds for the 2-Wasserstein distance between the DDPM sample andP ∗ under general Lipschitz-type conditions on the score function, and for a wide class of variance schedules, including the cosine schedule of [46] (Theorems 3.1–3.3). As a benchmark case, we illustrate our bound whenP ∗ is weakly log-concave in the sense of [22, 38] (Corollaries 3.1 and 3.2), demonstrating that it is optimal in both the dimension and the number of steps, and is tight in the sense that it vanishes for the standard normal distribution. Our results also recover several sharp error bounds recently obtained in the literature [2, 61, 65]. Second, we show that the DDPM satisfies a logarithmic Sobolev inequality when the learned score function satisfies a Lipschitz-type condition (Theorem 3.4). An important consequence of this result is that a 2-Wasserstein bound for the DDPM can be derived from the corresponding KL bound under a Lipschitz-type condition on the (learned) score, via the celebrated quadratic transportation cost inequality of Otto & Villani [49]. In particular, existing optimal 2-Wasserstein bounds for the DDPM can essentially be deduced from the recent sharp KL bound of [31] under geometric-type variance schedules. This raises the question of whether one can still achieve the optimal Wasserstein error bound even whenP ∗ does not satisfy a quadratic transportation cost inequality. Third, we provide an affirmative answer to this question in the general log-concave setting; see Theorems 3.5 and 3.6. Unlike the conventional approach, our analysis is based on interpreting the DDPM sampler as a dis- cretization of theF ¨ollmer process, rather than the reverse Ornstein–Uhlenbeck (OU) process. See Section 4.1 for the definition and key properties of the F ¨ollmer process, and [37] for a more detailed account. This per- spective offers several technical advantages. First, the drift

process of the F ¨ollmer process is a martingale, which simplifies the bias-variance decomposition of the sampling error compared to existing approaches; in particular, the bias term vanishes automatically. Second, the associated discretization step sizes are ef- fectively smaller than those in the reverse OU formulation, which allows us to consider variance schedules beyond the scope of standard theoretical analyses. Third, discretizating the F ¨ollmer process enables a more precise analysis of the initialization error in the DDPM. In particular, our results clarify that the performance 2

of the DDPM sampler is improved by appropriately choosing its initial mean. The rest of the paper is organized as follows. Section 2 provides a formal description of the DDPM and related concepts. Section 3 presents the main results of the paper. Section 4 demonstrates proofs of the main results, while Section A contains additional proofs for auxiliary results. Given constants0< β i <1fori∈ I N, suppose that we have a proxys i(x)of∇logπ ti(x), where ti := (1−δ) N−1Y j=i (1−β j)fori= 0,1, . . . , N−1,(2.1) andδ∈[0,1)is a constant. The caseδ >0corresponds to performingearly stopping. We sett N := 1−δ by convention. The DDPM sampler generates a sequence(Y i)N i=0 of random vectors inR d by the following recursion relation:    Y0 ∼N( √t0 ˆµ, Id), Yi+1 = 1√1−βi (Yi +β isi(Yi)) + √βiZi (i= 0,1, . . . , N−1), (2.2) whereˆµ∈R d is a prescribed initialization vector andZ i i.i.d.∼N(0, I d).ˆµis usually set to 0, but we will later see that choosingˆµclose toµreduces initialization errors. We also note that our indexing convention is the reverse of the usual one. Whenˆµ= 0, the sampler (2.2) coincides with the original sampling method of [26, 58] with the hyper- parameters optimized for the caseP ∗ =N(0, I d). Songet al.[59] refer to this procedure asancestral sampling. They also propose an alternative method, calledreverse diffusion sampling, which constructs a sampler via discretization of the reverse stochastic differential equation (SDE) in their SDE formulation of SGMs. For the DDPM, these two samplers become asymptotically equivalent whenmax i∈IN βi →0(see [59, Appendix E] and [16, Appendix B]). In this interpretation,βi approximately corresponds to thei-th step size of the discretization. By contrast, in our formulation the quantities hi :=t i+1 −t i, i= 0,1, . . . , N−1, serve as the effective step sizes. In particular, we do not necessarily requiremax i∈IN βi to be small, which allows us to consider variance schedules that lie beyond the scope of standard theoretical analyses in the literature. See Remarks 3.2 and 3.4 for further discussions. 2.1 Variance schedule The sequence(β i)N−1 i=0 is called thevariance scheduleor thenoise scheduleof the DDPM. On the one hand, it determines the variances of the Gaussian noise added to the data in the forward process. On the other hand, as discussed above, in the context of reverse diffusion sampling, it corresponds approximately to the step-size sequence of the discretization. The following two variance schedules are commonly used in theoretical analyses of the DDPM. Example 2.1(Constant schedule).We setβ i =β 0 for alli∈ IN. This variance schedule is used when the target distribution is sufficiently smooth and early stopping is not required; see, e.g., [10, 13]. Example 2.2(Geometric schedule).Given positive constantsc 0 andc 1, we set β0 = 1 N c0 , β i = c1 logN N min ( β0  1 + c1 logN N N−i ,1 ) (i= 1, . . . , N−1).(2.3) 4

This type of variance schedule is commonly used when deriving TV and KL error bounds in general settings with early stopping; see, e.g., [5, 10, 29, 31, 40, 41, 42, 43]. We then define(β i)N−1 i=0 by (2.4). We refer to [25] for other examples of variance schedules. 2.2 Assumptions on the learned score To control the approximation error of the learned score, we mainly impose the following assumption: [H] There exists a non-decreasing functionε score : (0,1)→[0,∞)such that ∥∇logπ ti(Yi)−s i(Yi)∥2 ≤ε score(ti)for alli∈ I N .(2.5) Note that theL 2-error on the left-hand side of (2.5) is evaluated with respect to the law ofY i, which itself depends ons i. While this type of assumption is commonly used in the analysis of 2-Wasserstein bounds (e.g., [2, 21, 22, 38, 62, 68]), it has been pointed out that it does not directly reflect the objective minimized during score training; see [6, Section 4] for instance. Indeed, the standard score-learning procedure implicitly minimizes∥∇logπ ti(ξ)−s i(ξ)∥2 withξ∼ StiP ∗ for everyi∈ IN; see, e.g., [48]. For this reason, some works instead impose the following alternative assumption [6, 50, 61, 65]: [H’] There exists a non-decreasing functionε score : (0,1)→[0,∞)such that ∥∇logπ ti(ξ)−s i(ξ)∥2 ≤ε score(ti)withξ∼ S tiP ∗ for alli∈ IN .(2.6) When working with [H’] instead of [H], one typically needs to impose additional regularity assumptions on the learned scores i; see the references cited above. For illustration, we will give a result of this type in Theorem 3.3. We also note thatε score is often assumed to be constant. Following [61], we allow it to depend on time. This is motivated by the fact thatε score(t)is typically expected to scale as(1−t) −1/2 according to [48, Theorem 3.1] (see also [60, Corollaries 4 and 5]). 1Our definition uses the sine function rather than the cosine function simply because our indexing convention is the reverse of that in [46]. 5

For a functionF: (0,1)×R d →R k such that the partial derivative ∂F ∂xj (t, x)∈R k exists at(t, x)∈(0,1)×R d for everyj= 1, . . . , d, we write ∇F(t, x) :=  ∂F ∂x1 (t, x). . . ∂F ∂xd (t, x)  ∈R k ⊗R d. Following [18], we call a continuous stochastic processX= (X t)t∈[0,1] inR d theF ¨ollmer processassociated withP ∗ ifX 1 ∼P ∗ and the conditional law ofXgivenX 1 is thed-dimensional Brownian bridge from 0 toX 1 on[0,1]. It is known thatXsolves the following SDE: dXt =v(t, Xt) dt+ dWt,(4.1) where v(t, x) = 1√ t ∇logf t  x√ t  .(4.2) 13

In fact, as shown in [37, Theorem 2.1],Xis always a weak solution to this SDE without any assumption on P ∗. To be precise, we have R 1 0 |v(t, Xt)|dt <∞a.s., and the process Wt :=X t − Z t 0 v(s, Xs) ds, t∈[0,1], is a standardF-Wiener process inR d, whereF= (F t)t∈[0,1] is the usual augmentation of the filtration generated byX(see [33, Definition 2.7.2] or [52, p.45]). Let us consider the Euler–Maruyama discretization of the SDE (4.1) over the time gridt 0 < t1 <· · ·< tN−1 < tN. It is given by    X N t0 :=t 0 ˆµ+Wt0, X N ti+1 :=X N ti +h iv(ti, XN ti ) +W ti+1 −W ti (i= 0,1, . . . , N−1). (4.3) Here, we useˆµas a proxy for the generally undefined quantityv(0,0). Replacing the score∇logπ ti by its proxys i, we define random vectors( ˆXti)N i=0 recursively by    ˆXt0 :=t 0 ˆµ+Wt0, ˆXti+1 :=  1 + hi ti  ˆXti + hi√ti si ˆXti√ti ! +W ti+1 −W ti (i= 0,1, . . . , N−1). A.4 Proof of Lemma 4.5 As pointed out in [53], an inequality of the form (4.10) is a consequence of the 2-smoothness of the underlying Banach space. In our setting, the latter corresponds to the following result. Lemma A.6.LetXandYbe two random vectors inR d. Then, for anyp≥2, ∥X+Y∥ 2 p +∥X−Y∥ 2 p ≤2∥X∥ 2 p + 2(p−1)∥Y∥ 2 p. Proof. This inequality follows as a special case of [63, Proposition 2.2]; see also [4, Theorem 1]. Proof of Lemma 4.5.We follow the proof of [28, Lemma A.1]. Without loss of generality, we may assume ∥X∥ p +∥Y∥ p <∞. Fixn∈Narbitrarily, and setZ:=n −1Y. Also, letD k :=∥X+kZ∥ 2 p − ∥X+ (k− 1)Z∥ 2 p −2k(p−1)∥Z∥ 2 p for every integerk. Since Lemma A.6 gives ∥X+kZ∥ 2 p +∥X+ (k−2)Z∥ 2 p ≤2∥X+ (k−1)Z∥ 2 p + 2(p−1)∥Z∥ 2 p, we haveD k ≤D k−1. Meanwhile, sinceE[Z|X] =n −1 E[Y|X] = 0, ∥X+Z∥ 2 p +∥X∥ 2 p =∥X+Z∥ 2 p +∥E[X−Z|X]∥ 2 p ≤ ∥X+Z∥2 p +∥X−Z∥ 2 p ≤2∥X∥ 2 p + 2(p−1)∥Z∥ 2 p, where the first inequality is by Jensen’s inequality and the second by Lemma A.6. Rearranging the terms givesD 1 ≤0. This yieldsD k ≤0for allk≥1. Using this inequality, we obtain ∥X+Y∥ 2 p − ∥X∥2 p = nX k=1 � Dk + 2k(p−1)∥Z∥ 2 p  ≤n(n+ 1)(p−1)∥Z∥ 2 p =  1 + 1 n  (p−1)∥Y∥ 2 p. Lettingn→ ∞gives the desired result. A.5 Proof of Lemma 4.12 We first prove (4.28) following the argument in [34, Section 7].
