A note on connections between the F¨ollmer process and the denoising diffusion probabilistic model Yuta Koike *† May 19, 2026 Abstract The F ¨ollmer process is a Brownian motion conditioned to have a pre-specified distribution at time 1. This process can be interpreted as an “augmented” time-compressed version of the reverse stochastic differential equation (SDE) for the denoising diffusion probabilistic model (DDPM). While this fact has been indirectly used to analyze DDPM sampling errors via discretization of the reverse SDE, connections between direct discretization of the F ¨ollmer process and the DDPM sampler have not yet been fully ex- plored. This note aims to clarify this point while surveying relevant results from existing work. We show that discretized F ¨ollmer processes give natural hyper-parameter settings of the DDPM sampler. More- over, this allows us to systematically recover state-of-the-art results on DDPM sampling error bounds with slight improvements. Keywords: diffusion model, discretization, low-dimensional adaptation, sampling error, score-based gen- erative modeling, stochastic localization. 1 Introduction Letµbe a probability distribution onR d. Following [17], we call ad-dimensional continuous stochastic processX= (X t)t∈[0,1] theF ¨ollmer processassociated withµifX 1 ∼µand the conditional law ofX givenX 1 is thed-dimensional Brownian bridge from 0 toX 1 on[0,1]. This process has recently received renewed attention in various fields of both theory and application such as studies of functional inequalities [16, 17, 18, 36, 38, 45, 46] as well as high-dimensional central limit theorems [19, 21, 22], optimization [9, 43], generative modeling [5, 59], sampling from probability distributions [20, 30, 55, 61] and Bayesian inference [52, 57]. Historically, the F ¨ollmer process appeared as the solution to an entropy minimization problem posed by Schr ¨odinger. A modern formulation of Schr ¨odinger’s problem is as follows (see [39, 44] for more details). LetW d :=C([0,1],R d)be the space of continuous functions from[0,1]toR d equipped with the uniform topology. For a probability distributionQonW d, we denote byQ 0 andQ 1 the image measures ofQby the mapsw7→w(0)andw7→w(1), respectively. Then, given a pair(µ 0, µ1)of probability distributions onR d and a probability distributionRonW d, thedynamical Schr ¨odinger problem associated with the reference measureRis the following minimization problem with respect to probability *Graduate School of Mathematical Sciences, University of Tokyo †CREST, Japan Science and Technology Agency 1 arXiv:2605.18040v1 [stat.ML] 18 May 2026

distributionsQonW d: min Q:Q0=µ0,Q1=µ1 H(Q|R).(1.1) Here, given two probability measuresPandQdefined on a common measurable space,H(P|Q)denotes the relative entropy ofPwith respect toQ, i.e. H(P|Q) = Z dP dQ log  dP dQ  dQifP≪Q andH(P|Q) =∞otherwise. F ¨ollmer [25] showed that whenH(µ|γ d)<∞withγ d =N(0, Id)the d-dimensional standard normal distribution, the law ofXgives the unique solution to (1.1) withµ 1 =µ,µ 0 being the unit mass at 0 andRbeing thed-dimensional Wiener measure; see Section II.1.3 ibidem. Under the finite entropy conditionH(µ|γ d)<∞, it follows from F ¨ollmer [24]’s results thatXis a weak solution to the following stochastic differential equation (SDE): dXt =  Xt t +∇logp t(Xt)  dt+dW t (0≤t≤1), X 0 = 0,(1.2) whereW= (W t)t∈[0,1] is a standard Wiener process inR d and for every0< t <1,p t denotes the density ofX t, i.e. pt(x) = 1 {2πt(1−t)} d/2 Z Rd exp  − |x−ty| 2 2t(1−t)  µ(dy), x∈R d. See Remark 2.2 for details and also [10, 33] for related results. Indeed, we will show thatXalways solves (1.2) withoutanyassumption onµ; see Theorem 2.1.∇logp t is known as thescore functionof (the law of) Xt, and the form of (1.2) reminds us of the SDE formulation ofscore-based generative modeling(SGM) in Songet al.[54]. In fact, it turns out thatXis an “augmented” time-compressed version of the reverse SDE corresponding to thedenoising diffusion probabilistic model(DDPM) of [29]; see Proposition 3.1. Although this fact is essentially known in the literature (see the discussion preceding Proposition 3.1), existing work does not seem to fully explore connections between discretization of the F ¨ollmer process and the DDPM sampler. This note aims to clarify this point while surveying relevant results in the literature. As we will see in Section 3, discretized F ¨ollmer processes give natural hyper-parameter settings of the DDPM sampler. In addition, we can systematically recover state-of-the-art results on DDPM sampling error bounds in [3, 8, 31], along with slight improvements. The key to our development is the fact that the F ¨ollmer process is strongly linked to some information-theoretic quantities and Eldan [15]’s stochastic localization. Indeed, these links were implicitly used in [3, 31] to analyze discretization errors of the reverse SDE in SGM. Our development complements these works. While the F ¨ollmer process has been already used for generative modeling as mentioned above, most studies have used it for developing an alternative model to the DDPM rather than analyzing the DDPM itself. In fact, Chenet al.[5] and Wanget al.[59] employ the F ¨ollmer process as a part of more sophisticated generative models proposed therein. We also remark that a solution to the problem (1.1) in the general case, known as theSchr ¨odinger bridge, is extensively employed for generative modeling. Among others, its 2

connection to SGM is discussed in [4, 6, 12, 49]. However, the focus of these studies is to develop a more general framework than SGM.1 The remainder of this note is organized as follows. Section 2 reviews basic properties of the F ¨ollmer process. Section 3 discusses its connection to the DDPM, with emphasis on its implication to sampling error evaluation. The appendix presents additional mathematical proofs. NotationFor a vectorx∈R d, we denote the Euclidean norm ofxby|x|. Ifξis a random vector in Rd,P ξ denotes the law ofξ. In particular,P W denotes the d-dimensional Wiener measure. 2 Properties of the F ¨ollmer process In this section, we review basic properties of the F ¨ollmer process. Although most results are known in the literature at least under additional regularity onµ, we give their proofs to avoid such extra assumptions. To fix the terminology, we define the F¨ollmer process as follows: Definition 2.1(F ¨ollmer process).Forx, y∈R d, we writeP y x for the law of the Brownian bridge fromxtoy induced onW d. Ad-dimensional continuous stochastic processX= (X t)t∈[0,1] is called aF ¨ollmer process associated withµif its law induced onW d is equal to R Rd P y 0 µ(dy). We can realize a F ¨ollmer process associated withµasX t =tξ+β t,t∈[0,1], whereξ∼µand β= (β t)t∈[0,1] is a standard Brownian bridge on[0,1]independent ofξ. The next proposition shows that any F¨ollmer process is realized in this way: Proposition 2.1.LetX= (X t)t∈[0,1] be a F ¨ollmer process associated withµ. Define a processβ= (βt)t∈[0,1] asβ t =X t −tX 1,t∈[0,1]. Thenβis a standard Brownian bridge independent ofX 1. Proof. By definition, the conditional law ofβgivenX 1 is a standard Brownian bridge inR d. Since this conditional law does not depend onX 1, the unconditional law ofβis also a standard Brownian bridge and βis independent ofX 1. In the remainder of this note,X= (X t)t∈[0,1] always denotes a F ¨ollmer process associated withµand β= (β t)t∈[0,1] is defined as in Proposition 2.1 unless otherwise is stated. Proposition 2.2.Xis a Markov process. Proof. Since the law of the time reversal process ˜X= (X 1−t)t∈[0,1] is R Rd P 0 x µ(dx), ˜Xis a Markov process with the same transition function as the Brownian bridge and initial distributionµ. Then, by property (iii) in 1Note that Chen & Vanden-Eijnden [6] use the term “F¨ollmer process” to indicate the Schr¨odinger bridge whose initial marginal µ0 is the Dirac measure. 3

3 Connection to the denoising diffusion probabilistic model We begin by introducing the SDE formulation of the DDPM formally. LetY= (Y t)t≥0 be a solution to the following SDE: dYt =−Y tdt+ √ 2dWt, Y 0 ∼µ.(3.1) ForT >0, let Y= ( Y t)t∈[0,T] be the time reversal of(Y t)t∈[0,T] , i.e. Y t =Y T−t fort∈[0, T]. Then, by [28, Theorem 2.1], [51, Proposition VII.2.4] and L ´evy’s characterization, there exists a standard Wiener process W= ( W t)t∈[0,T) inR d such that Y t = Y 0 + Z t 0 � Y s + 2∇logq T−s (Y s)  ds+ √ 2W t (3.2) for allt∈[0, T), whereq t is the density ofY t for everyt >0. As already mentioned in the introduction, the F ¨ollmer process can be seen as an “augmented” time- compressed version of the reverse SDE (3.2). This fact is essentially known in the literature, as the F ¨ollmer process is an (augmented) time-compressed version of Eldan’s stochastic localization process (see [37, Sec- tion 4.2]) and the process Yin (3.2) is a change of variables of the latter (see [47, Section 1.3] or [53, Theorem 3]). Hence the process fWt = 1√ 2 Z t 0 eT−s dVs (t∈[0, T]) is an(F X e2(t−T) )-Wiener process inR d by L´evy’s characterization. SinceF X e2(t−T) =F Y t for allt∈[0, T], we complete the proof once we show that W t = fWt for everyt∈[0, T]. Observe thatq t(y) =p e−2t(e−ty)fort >0andy∈R d. Lemma A.1(Tweedie’s formula).LetYandZbe independent random vectors inR d such thatZ∼N(0,Σ) for some positive definite symmetric matrixΣ. ThenY+Zhas a positive, smooth densityf Y+Z and ∇logf Y+Z (Y+Z) =−E[Σ −1Z|Y+Z]. Proof. It is well-known thatf Y+Z (x) = E[ϕΣ(x−Y)]is the density ofY+Z. So the first claim follows. We also obtain E[|∇logf Y+Z (Y+Z)|] = Z Rd |∇fY+Z (x)|dx≤E Z Rd |∇ϕΣ(x−Y)|dx  = Z Rd |∇ϕΣ(x)|dx <∞. Hence the second claim follows once we show that E[∇logf Y+Z (Y+Z)g(Y+Z)] =−E[Σ −1Zg(Y+Z)] for any compactly supported smooth functiong:R d →R. Integration by parts gives E[∇logf Y+Z (Y+Z)g(Y+Z)] = Z Rd ∇fY+Z (x)g(x)dx=− Z Rd fY+Z (x)∇g(x)dx. Meanwhile, by the multivariate Stein identity E[Σ−1Zg(Y+Z)] = E[∇g(Y+Z)] = Z Rd ∇g(x)fY+Z (x)dx.
